proposals.title: Longitudinal Assessment of Nocturnal Prolactin Secretion as a State Marker of Depressive Episodes in Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research implicates dysregulated prolactin (PRL) secretion in adolescent depression, but it is unclear if this is a stable trait vulnerability or a transient state marker of acute illness. Clarifying this distinction is critical for understanding the pathophysiology of depression and for identifying biomarkers that track clinical course, which could inform treatment timing and relapse prediction. This directly extends Dahl's prior work on HPA axis and cortisol dynamics in adolescent affective disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: Nocturnal prolactin rhythm abnormalities (e.g., elevated peak, altered timing) will be significantly associated with the presence and severity of a current major depressive episode (state), but will normalize upon clinical remission, and will not be a stable trait observable in high-risk adolescents during euthymic periods.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal case-control study with three groups: depressed adolescents (n=60), remitted/high-risk adolescents (n=60), and healthy controls (n=60). Participants will undergo intensive sampling of plasma prolactin every 20 minutes for 24 hours at baseline and, for the depressed group, again after 8-12 weeks of treatment. We will model PRL time-series using cosinor analysis to derive rhythm parameters (mesor, amplitude, acrophase). We will use mixed-effects models to test within-subject change with symptom improvement (PHQ-9). Trait stability will be assessed by comparing the remitted/high-risk group (euthymic) to controls.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first longitudinal evidence differentiating state vs. trait characteristics of prolactin dysregulation in adolescent depression. It will advance the neuroendocrine model of depression by linking a specific hormone rhythm to clinical state. The findings could establish prolactin dynamics as a useful biomarker for monitoring treatment response.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has shown cross-sectional PRL differences, no study has prospectively tracked its rhythm across the acute and remission phases of adolescent depression to formally test its state dependency. The integration of intensive time-series analysis with a longitudinal high-risk design moves beyond static hormone measures.

proposals.title: Stress-Induced Disruption of Normative Amygdala-HPA Axis Development in Adolescence: A Longitudinal Study of Risk for Affective Disorders
proposals.motivation: Prior work by Dahl and others has identified altered HPA axis function and amygdala reactivity in adolescents with or at risk for depression, often interpreting these as biomarkers of the disorder. However, given the profound neurodevelopmental changes in limbic and stress systems during adolescence, these patterns may instead represent a stress-induced deviation from a typical developmental trajectory, not a disease-specific signature. Clarifying this is crucial for developing accurate predictive models and targeted interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the patterns of HPA axis dysregulation and heightened amygdala reactivity observed in depressed adolescents represent an acceleration or distortion of normative adolescent development under chronic stress, rather than a pathophysiological state specific to major depressive disorder.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing longitudinal data (e.g., from the Pittsburgh Girls Study) tracking cortisol secretion (diurnal and stress-reactive) and amygdala reactivity (fMRI to emotional faces) from early to late adolescence. We will use mixed-effects growth curve modeling to establish normative developmental trajectories. We will then apply **mediation and moderation analyses** to test if early life stress exposure explains the deviation of the depressed group's trajectory from the normative path. Key comparisons will be between depressed, high-stress non-depressed, and low-stress non-depressed adolescents.
proposals.contribution: This study will shift the conceptual framework from seeking depression-specific biomarkers to identifying how stress perturbs typical adolescent neurodevelopment, creating a pathway to psychopathology. It will provide a more nuanced model for risk prediction by integrating developmental timing and stress exposure. The findings could reorient prevention efforts towards mitigating developmental disruptions rather than treating presumed disease states.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the standard disease-model assumption by explicitly testing whether the observed physiological patterns are better explained by a stress-disrupted normative development model. It directly integrates Dahl's work on HPA axis, amygdala, and adolescent development to challenge a core interpretation in the field.

proposals.title: Disentangling Neuroendocrine Pathways: A Multi-System Pharmaco-fMRI Study of Prolactin Response in Adolescents with Affective Risk
proposals.motivation: Prolactin secretion is a key neuroendocrine marker, often used as an indirect readout of central dopaminergic tone in studies of stress and affective disorders. However, pre-2026 evidence from Ronald E. Dahl's work and others shows that serotonin (e.g., TPH2 variants influencing amygdala reactivity) and GABA systems are also dysregulated in adolescent psychopathology and potently modulate prolactin. Assuming prolactin differences solely reflect dopamine may lead to incorrect inferences about neural pathophysiology in at-risk youth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individual differences in prolactin response to a standardized challenge will be more strongly predicted by integrated measures of serotonin and GABA system reactivity, assessed via genetic and neuroimaging biomarkers, than by a model considering dopaminergic proxies alone.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 adolescents (ages 12-16) with familial loading for affective disorder. Participants will undergo a double-blind, placebo-controlled, within-subject pharmaco-fMRI challenge across three sessions, using subclinical doses of selective agents: a dopamine antagonist (amisulpride), a serotonin agonist (m-CPP), and a GABA-A modulator (alprazolam). Primary outcomes are prolactin area-under-the-curve and amygdala reactivity (BOLD signal) during an emotional faces task. We will use mixed-effects models with random intercepts to analyze within-subject effects. Mediation analysis will test whether amygdala reactivity (a pre-2026 neural target for both serotonin and stress) mediates the drug effect on prolactin. Pre-existing genetic variants (e.g., TPH2) will be included as moderators.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, within-subject comparison of how major neurotransmitter systems govern a canonical neuroendocrine marker in a developmental risk population. It will clarify the specific neurobiological pathways (dopaminergic vs. serotonergic/GABAergic) underlying HPA-axis-related endocrine dysregulation, offering more precise targets for early intervention. The findings will refine the interpretation of prolactin as a biomarker in developmental psychopathology.
proposals.novelty: While prolactin is routinely used as a dopamine proxy, no pre-2026 study has systematically challenged this assumption by directly comparing the relative contributions of dopamine, serotonin, and GABA using a controlled pharmaco-neuroimaging design in a developmental sample characterized by affective risk.

proposals.title: Dissociating Circadian and Sleep-Wake Drivers of the Prolactin Rhythm in Adolescents: Implications for Stress and Metabolic Risk
proposals.motivation: Prolactin secretion exhibits a robust nocturnal rise, traditionally attributed to sleep itself. However, this assumption obscures potential independent circadian regulation, a critical distinction for understanding endocrine dysregulation in adolescent populations at risk for affective disorders and obesity. Clarifying the primary drivers of prolactin rhythm is essential for developing targeted interventions for stress-related endocrine dysfunction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the 24-hour prolactin profile in adolescents is not merely secondary to sleep architecture but is governed by a significant endogenous circadian component that can be dissociated from sleep-wake states under controlled conditions.
proposals.method: We will employ a rigorous within-subject, forced desynchrony protocol in a controlled laboratory setting with healthy adolescents and those with familial risk for affective disorders. Frequent blood sampling will measure prolactin, cortisol, and growth hormone across multiple ~28-hour 'days' to separate circadian (~24-h) from sleep-wake (~1/3) influences. Time-series analyses will use cosinor and linear mixed-effects models to partition variance, with specific attention to the interaction between circadian phase, sleep stages (polysomnography), and stress system markers (HPA axis).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence in adolescents quantifying the relative contributions of circadian timing versus sleep to prolactin secretion. It will establish a foundational model for how sleep-circadian misalignment may disrupt prolactin signaling, offering a novel mechanistic pathway linking disrupted sleep to stress vulnerability and metabolic risk (e.g., obesity) in development.
proposals.novelty: While prolactin's link to sleep is well-documented, explicitly testing its circadian independence in a developmental high-risk cohort using a forced desynchrony paradigm is novel. This moves beyond correlation to causal attribution of rhythm generation, integrating Dr. Dahl's expertise in adolescent stress, sleep, and HPA axis function.

proposals.title: Ecological Discrepancy in HPA-Axis and Sleep Physiology: Comparing 24-Hour Inpatient Profiles to Ambulatory Real-World Patterns in Adolescents with Affective Risk
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Dahl's own work, heavily relies on inpatient or lab-based 24-hour sampling of cortisol, growth hormone, and sleep to characterize dysregulation in adolescents at risk for mood disorders and obesity. However, the controlled setting lacks the naturalistic stressors and daily routines that may critically shape these secretory patterns. This gap limits the ecological validity of our pathophysiological models and their predictive power for real-world outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that key physiological metrics—specifically the diurnal slope of cortisol, nocturnal growth hormone secretion, and sleep architecture parameters—measured in a controlled inpatient setting will show systematic and clinically meaningful discrepancies from patterns measured under normal daily life conditions in the same individuals. Further, the magnitude of this discrepancy will be associated with individual differences in amygdala reactivity to threat.
proposals.method: We will recruit 60 adolescents (ages 12-16) with familial loading for affective disorder. Each will undergo: 1) a 24-hour inpatient stay with frequent salivary cortisol sampling and polysomnography, and 2) a 7-day ambulatory assessment at home using a wearable device for sleep (actigraphy) and a programmed smartphone to prompt 6 saliva samples per day. We will use mixed-effects models to compare within-subject profiles across settings. The primary analysis will model the diurnal cortisol slope (calculated using the 'time' of sample and 'median' cortisol per period) 'between' settings. We will also compute the 'normal' nighttime area-under-the-curve for growth hormone-linked sleep stages (SWS) and compare it across settings. Amygdala reactivity, measured via fMRI to fearful faces, will be tested as a moderator of the setting discrepancy.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly quantify the 'setting effect' bias in core psychobiological markers, providing a crucial calibration for interpreting decades of inpatient/lab-based research. It will identify which physiological parameters are most context-sensitive, improving the design of future ecologically valid studies. Findings could refine predictive models of clinical outcome by incorporating context-dependent physiological reactivity.
proposals.novelty: While the limitation of lab settings is often acknowledged, this study provides the first within-subject, multi-system quantitative comparison of 24-hour profiles across settings in a high-risk adolescent cohort, explicitly linking the discrepancy to a neural phenotype (amygdala reactivity) implicated in stress sensitivity.

proposals.title: Differentiating Adolescent Depressive Subtypes: A 24-Hour Study of Prolactin Secretory Rhythms in Melancholic vs. Atypical Presentations
proposals.motivation: Adolescent depression is a heterogeneous syndrome with distinct neurobiological underpinnings. Melancholic and atypical subtypes show opposing neurovegetative symptoms (e.g., insomnia/hypersomnia, appetite loss/gain), suggesting divergent hypothalamic dysregulation. Prolactin, a stress-sensitive hormone with a robust circadian rhythm influenced by hypothalamic monoamines, provides a unique window into these subtype-specific neuroendocrine pathways, which remain poorly characterized in youth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents with melancholic depression will exhibit a blunted and phase-advanced 24-hour prolactin secretory rhythm, while those with atypical depression will show an elevated and phase-delayed rhythm, reflecting their opposing symptom profiles and underlying monoaminergic dysfunction.
proposals.method: We will recruit 60 depressed adolescents (30 melancholic, 30 atypical, diagnosed via K-SADS-PL) and 30 healthy controls. Participants will undergo a precise 24-hour hormonal sampling protocol in a controlled laboratory setting, with blood drawn every 20-30 minutes via an indwelling catheter. Using deconvolution analysis, we will calculate pulsatile secretion parameters (e.g., pulse mass, frequency). We will then apply functional data analysis (FDA) and non-linear mixed-effects models to compare the 24-hour trajectories (mesor, amplitude, acrophase) between groups, controlling for sex, age, and BMI, as in Dahl's prior cortisol work.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed characterization of 24-hour prolactin dynamics in adolescent depressive subtypes, linking specific neuroendocrine signatures to clinical phenotypes. It will advance a biophysiological model for subtype differentiation and identify potential biomarkers for predicting treatment response and long-term outcomes, such as obesity risk in atypical depression.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined cortisol and growth hormone in adolescent depression, the 24-hour secretory pattern of prolactin—a direct marker of hypothalamic serotonin and dopamine tone—has not been systematically compared across melancholic and atypical subtypes, offering a novel test of their opposing neurobiological models.

proposals.title: Deconvolution Analysis of Nocturnal Prolactin Secretion in Adolescents: Linking Pulsatile Dynamics to Stress Reactivity and Metabolic Risk
proposals.motivation: Prolactin, a stress-responsive hormone, is implicated in metabolic regulation and emotional processing, yet its pulsatile secretion dynamics in youth are poorly characterized. Pre-2026 research by Dahl and others links stress, sleep, and HPA axis function to adolescent risk for obesity and affective disorders, but the role of prolactin's secretory *pattern* remains unexplored. Quantifying its pulsatile architecture could reveal a novel endocrine mechanism connecting chronic stress to metabolic and psychiatric vulnerability during this developmental window.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents with higher familial risk for affective disorders or obesity will exhibit altered nocturnal prolactin secretory burst mass and frequency, and that these pulsatile dynamics will correlate with measures of amygdala reactivity and attenuated growth hormone secretion.
proposals.method: We will apply validated hormone pulse detection algorithms, specifically the deconvolution method of Veldhuis and Johnson (1992) and the Cluster algorithm, to existing high-frequency (every 10-20 min) nocturnal prolactin time series data from well-phenotyped adolescent cohorts. This analysis will quantify secretory burst mass, frequency, and half-life. These pulsatility metrics will then be analyzed using mixed-effects models to test associations with pre-existing measures of 24-hour cortisol secretion, amygdala reactivity (from fMRI), growth hormone profiles, and clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed characterization of prolactin pulsatility in adolescents at risk for psychopathology, moving beyond mean hormone levels to dynamic secretion. It will establish novel endocrine links between stress system dysregulation, limbic brain function, and metabolic risk. The findings could identify prolactin pulsatility as a potential biomarker for targeted intervention in developmental trajectories toward obesity and mood disorders.
proposals.novelty: While deconvolution is established in endocrinology for hormones like LH and GH, its application to prolactin dynamics in developmental psychopathology is novel. Integrating these quantitative endocrine metrics with neural (amygdala) and clinical stress phenotypes bridges methodological domains rarely combined in adolescent research.

proposals.title: Linking Motor Activity Patterns to Prolactin Secretion Dynamics in Adolescents: An Actigraphy and Hormonal Sampling Study
proposals.motivation: Prolactin, a hormone sensitive to stress and sleep, plays a key role in metabolic and affective regulation, with implications for obesity and mood disorders. However, its secretion patterns in relation to naturalistic, free-living motor activity and rest in youth are poorly characterized. Understanding this link is critical for developmental models of stress-related pathophysiology, given Ronald Dahl's prior work on HPA axis dysfunction, cortisol, and affective risk in adolescents.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific actigraphy-derived metrics of rest fragmentation and nocturnal activity will be temporally coupled with, and predictive of, the amplitude and timing of prolactin secretion peaks in adolescents, particularly in those with familial risk for affective disorders.
proposals.method: We will recruit 60 adolescents (12-16 years) with varying familial loading for affective disorders. Participants will wear a research-grade wrist actigraph (e.g., Actiwatch Spectrum) for 7 consecutive days and nights. On the final 24-hour period, they will undergo frequent salivary sampling (every 30-60 minutes) to assay prolactin. Actigraphy data will be processed using validated algorithms (e.g., Sadeh, Cole-Kripke) to derive metrics: sleep onset latency, wake after sleep onset (WASO), fragmentation index, and daytime activity counts. Time-series analyses (cross-correlation, multilevel modeling) will examine temporal relationships between activity/rest transitions and prolactin levels.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated, high-resolution description of how real-world motor behavior links to prolactin secretion in youth. It will clarify a potential mechanistic pathway connecting disrupted rest-activity cycles (a transdiagnostic feature) to endocrine dysregulation relevant for obesity and mood disorders. The findings could inform novel biomarkers for early risk detection based on actigraphy.
proposals.novelty: While actigraphy and hormone sampling are established, their integrated use to capture the dynamic, within-subject coupling of motor activity and prolactin in a developmental, high-risk sample is novel. It moves beyond static, between-subject comparisons to model temporal processes, aligning with Dahl's recent focus on time-series analysis of cortisol.

proposals.title: Quantifying Circadian Dysregulation of Prolactin in Adolescents with Familial Risk for Affective Disorders: A Cosinor Analysis of 24-Hour Profiles
proposals.motivation: Prolactin secretion follows a robust circadian rhythm, peaking during sleep, and is sensitive to stress and hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis function. Pre-2026 work by Dahl and others has established that adolescents at familial risk for affective disorders show HPA axis dysregulation and altered stress reactivity, which may disrupt circadian hormone rhythms. Precisely quantifying this disruption in prolactin—a hormone linked to stress, sleep, and metabolic function—could reveal a novel biomarker of vulnerability preceding clinical disorder onset.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents with a familial loading for affective disorder will exhibit significant alterations in the circadian rhythm of prolactin secretion—specifically a reduced amplitude and a phase delay—compared to healthy controls, and that these rhythm parameters will correlate with measures of stress reactivity and subclinical depressive symptoms.
proposals.method: We will apply single- and population-mean cosinor analysis, a standard chronobiological method, to model 24-hour serum prolactin profiles obtained via frequent sampling (e.g., every 20-30 minutes) in an existing or newly recruited cohort of adolescents. The analysis will fit a cosine curve (Y = M + A*cos(ωt + φ)) to each participant's data to derive the mesor (M, rhythm-adjusted mean), amplitude (A, half the peak-trough difference), and acrophase (φ, peak time). These parameters will be compared between high-risk and control groups using ANOVA, and correlated with pre-existing measures of amygdala reactivity (from fMRI), cortisol profiles, and clinical symptomatology.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first precise, quantitative characterization of prolactin circadian dysregulation in adolescents at familial risk for mood disorders. It will directly link Dahl's prior work on HPA axis dysfunction and stress neurobiology to a specific, measurable disturbance in a sleep-related endocrine rhythm. The findings could identify a novel, quantifiable endocrine target for early intervention or for tracking treatment response.
proposals.novelty: While cosinor analysis is established in chronobiology, its application to model prolactin rhythms in the specific context of adolescent affective disorder risk—and its integration with neural (amygdala) and stress (cortisol) biomarkers from Dahl's prior research—is novel and moves beyond descriptive hormone levels to a formal analysis of rhythm dynamics.

proposals.title: Dopaminergic Modulation of Circadian Reward Sensitivity and Stress Reactivity in Adolescents with Obesity Risk: A Pharmacologic Challenge Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dahl and others links adolescent obesity to disrupted sleep-wake cycles, stress system dysregulation, and altered reward processing, but the underlying neurochemical drivers remain poorly understood. Pharmacologic challenge paradigms, proven effective in probing serotonin and HPA axis function in mood disorders, have not been systematically applied to disentangle how baseline circadian rhythm differences in at-risk youth relate to dopaminergic system sensitivity, a key pathway for motivation and reward.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents with obesity risk and evening circadian preference will exhibit a blunted behavioral and neural response to a dopamine agonist challenge, reflecting reduced dopaminergic system sensitivity, and that this blunted response will correlate with attenuated diurnal cortisol rhythms and increased amygdala reactivity to food cues.
proposals.method: We will recruit 60 adolescents (ages 14-16), stratified by obesity risk and circadian preference (Morningness-Eveningness Questionnaire). In a double-blind, placebo-controlled, crossover design, participants will undergo challenge sessions with a low-dose dopamine D2 receptor agonist (e.g., bromocriptine) and placebo. Primary outcomes include: 1) behavioral reward sensitivity (probabilistic reward task), 2) neural reactivity (fMRI during food cue and monetary incentive delay tasks focusing on ventral striatum and amygdala), and 3) diurnal cortisol secretion (8 saliva samples over 24h). Data will be analyzed using linear mixed-effects models to test for group-by-challenge condition interactions, and mediation analyses will examine pathways linking dopaminergic response, cortisol rhythm, and neural reactivity.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence for dopaminergic system sensitivity as a mechanistic link between circadian rhythms and obesity risk in youth. It will move the field beyond correlational observations by using a causal pharmacologic probe, offering a novel neurobiological target for preventive interventions aimed at normalizing reward and stress systems in adolescence.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of a controlled dopamine challenge with multi-system measurement (HPA axis, neural reward circuitry) in a developmental, high-risk sample, directly testing a neurochemical mechanism for established circadian-behavioral phenotypes.

proposals.title: Population-Level Salivary Prolactin Rhythms as a Biomarker for Adolescent Depression Risk: An Epidemiological Cohort Study
proposals.motivation: Prolactin exhibits a robust diurnal rhythm and is implicated in stress response and affective regulation, with dysregulation observed in depression. However, existing studies are small-scale and rely on invasive blood sampling, limiting understanding of its population-level relevance. Large-scale, non-invasive assessment of prolactin rhythm could identify a novel, modifiable biomarker for depression risk in youth, a critical pre-2026 public health priority.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a flattened diurnal rhythm of salivary prolactin, characterized by a reduced amplitude and altered timing, will be prospectively associated with increased risk for incident depressive symptoms in a population-based adolescent cohort.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing large epidemiological cohorts (e.g., ABCD Study) to collect passive diurnal salivary prolactin samples (waking, +30 min, bedtime) over a 3-day period in ~5000 adolescents. Salivary prolactin will be assayed using established ELISA kits. Diurnal rhythm parameters (amplitude, mesor, acrophase) will be derived using cosinor analysis. We will use mixed-effects models to test associations between rhythm parameters and concurrent/internalizing symptoms, and Cox proportional hazards models to predict new-onset depression over a 2-year follow-up, controlling for known confounders (sleep, BMI, cortisol).
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first large-scale, normative data on the diurnal salivary prolactin rhythm in adolescents. It will establish the utility of a low-cost, non-invasive biomarker for stratifying depression risk at a population level. The findings could inform early intervention strategies targeting neuroendocrine dysregulation.
proposals.novelty: While cortisol rhythms are well-studied, this is the first proposal to scale up the measurement of the prolactin rhythm—a hormone closely tied to stress and amygdala function—using non-invasive methods in a population cohort. It moves beyond small clinical samples to test a community-level biomarker hypothesis for adolescent depression.

proposals.title: Specificity of 24-Hour Neuroendocrine and Sleep-Wake Rhythms in Adolescent Anxiety, OCD, and ADHD: A Multi-Disorder Chronobiological Profiling Study
proposals.motivation: Prior work by Dahl and others has established that depressed adolescents exhibit distinct 24-hour cortisol secretion profiles and sleep-wake rhythm disturbances, which may predict long-term clinical outcomes. However, it remains unclear whether these chronobiological disruptions are specific to depression or represent a transdiagnostic marker of adolescent psychopathology. Testing this specificity is crucial for developing targeted, biologically-informed interventions and understanding shared versus disorder-specific pathophysiology in the developing brain.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents with Major Depressive Disorder will show a unique pattern of 24-hour rhythm dysregulation—characterized by evening cortisol elevation and disrupted sleep architecture—that is distinct from the profiles observed in anxiety disorders, OCD, and ADHD, despite overlapping clinical symptoms.
proposals.method: We will recruit 200 adolescents (aged 12-17), with 40 each diagnosed with MDD, an anxiety disorder, OCD, ADHD, and 40 healthy controls. All participants will undergo intensive 24-hour biological profiling: hourly salivary cortisol sampling, continuous actigraphy for sleep-wake patterns, and serial self-reported mood/energy ratings. Primary analyses will use mixed-effects growth curve models to compare diurnal cortisol slopes (calculated using piecewise regression) and cosinor analysis to model circadian parameters (mesor, amplitude, acrophase) across groups. Secondary analyses will use mediation models to test whether sleep midpoint and fragmentation (derived from actigraphy) explain group differences in cortisol rhythms.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct, multi-disorder comparison of 24-hour neuroendocrine and behavioral rhythms in adolescent psychopathology. It will clarify whether previously observed rhythm disruptions are biomarkers specific to depression or general markers of distress. The findings will inform the development of disorder-specific chronobiological treatments (e.g., timing of psychotherapy or medication) and refine neurodevelopmental models of stress system dysregulation.
proposals.novelty: While 24-hour cortisol profiling has been applied to depressed adolescents, its systematic application and comparison across internalizing (anxiety, OCD) and externalizing (ADHD) disorders in adolescence is novel. The integration of cortisol cosinor analysis with actigraphy-derived sleep metrics in a transdiagnostic adolescent sample represents a significant methodological advance beyond single-disorder studies.

proposals.title: Genetic and Early Adversity Contributions to Amygdala-Prefrontal Circuit Dysfunction in Adolescents at Risk for Affective and Metabolic Disorders
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dahl and others has established that altered amygdala reactivity and connectivity, often linked to stress-axis dysfunction, are neural risk factors for adolescent-onset depression and obesity. However, the specific etiological pathways—whether driven by genetic susceptibility (e.g., in serotonin or stress-regulatory genes) or by exposure to early life stress (ELS)—that lead to these circuit abnormalities remain poorly differentiated. Understanding these distinct pathways is critical for developing targeted, mechanism-based prevention strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific genetic variants (e.g., in TPH2, FKBP5) will be associated with intrinsic amygdala-prefrontal connectivity profiles, whereas ELS exposure will be uniquely linked to stress-system (HPA axis) dysregulation which, in turn, will mediate its effects on circuit function and subsequent affective/metabolic outcomes.
proposals.method: We will recruit a longitudinal cohort of 200 adolescents (ages 12-14) with familial loading for affective disorders. Participants will undergo resting-state and emotion-task fMRI to quantify amygdala-subgenual anterior cingulate and amygdala-ventromedial prefrontal cortex connectivity. We will genotype pre-specified risk variants (TPH2 rs4570625, FKBP5 rs1360780) and obtain detailed measures of ELS using the Childhood Trauma Questionnaire and interview-based measures. Analyses will use median-split and between-group comparisons for genetic and ELS factors, followed by moderated mediation models (PROCESS) to test for interactive and indirect effects of ELS via cortisol awakening response and growth hormone secretion on connectivity over time.
proposals.contribution: This study will directly test and potentially disentangle genetic versus stress-mediated pathways to a key neural risk phenotype. It will advance the field by linking specific etiological factors to circuit-level dysfunction, informing more precise biotypes within adolescent affective and metabolic risk. The findings could guide early identification and differentiate targets for pharmacological (genetic) versus psychosocial (stress) interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined genes or stress separately, this proposal integrates them within a longitudinal, multi-system framework (neural, endocrine, genetic) to test their unique and interactive contributions to circuit development, moving beyond simple association to mechanistic pathway modeling.

proposals.title: Differential Impact of Sleep, Stress, and Physical Activity on Chronic Pain in Adolescents: A Multimodal, Longitudinal Study
proposals.motivation: Chronic pain in adolescents is a major public health concern with poorly understood etiology. Pre-2026 research shows lifestyle factors like sleep, stress, and physical activity are linked to pain, but studies often examine them in isolation, assuming equal contribution. This fails to identify primary, modifiable targets for intervention, particularly in youth where neurodevelopment and stress systems are highly plastic.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific lifestyle factors, particularly sleep disruption and stress-related hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis dysfunction, are dominant drivers of chronic pain in adolescents, exerting a stronger influence than other factors like physical activity or diet.
proposals.method: We will recruit 200 adolescents (12-17 years) with and without chronic pain for a 12-month longitudinal study. Lifestyle factors (sleep via actigraphy, perceived stress, physical activity, diet) and pain metrics will be assessed quarterly. We will use multilevel modeling to analyze the time-lagged, between-person and within-person effects of each factor on subsequent pain. Dominance analysis and relative weight analysis will statistically compare the unique predictive contribution of each lifestyle factor, controlling for demographics and affective symptoms. Amygdala reactivity to negative stimuli (from fMRI) and diurnal cortisol (AUCg, slope) will be tested as neurobiological mediators of the sleep/stress-pain link.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field from a generic 'lifestyle matters' model to a mechanistic, hierarchical understanding of pain etiology in youth. It will identify the most potent lifestyle target for preventive interventions. By integrating behavioral assessment with neural (amygdala) and endocrine (HPA axis) measures, it will elucidate the pathways through which dominant factors like sleep exert their influence.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in directly testing the assumption of equal contribution among lifestyle factors using robust statistical comparison of their predictive dominance, and in grounding this test in the developmental neuroscience of stress-sleep-emotion circuits, a core focus of Dr. Dahl's pre-2026 work.

proposals.title: Digital Phenotyping of Lifestyle and Stress in Adolescent Chronic Pain: A Smartphone Sensor and Ecological Momentary Assessment Study
proposals.motivation: Chronic pain in adolescents is a debilitating condition with strong links to stress, sleep disruption, and affective disorders, yet objective, real-world measures of contributing lifestyle factors are lacking. Digital phenotyping, successfully applied in psychiatry to quantify behavior and mood, offers a novel framework to capture the dynamic interplay between daily activities, stress physiology, and pain perception in youths. This approach directly addresses gaps in understanding how modifiable lifestyle factors contribute to pain chronicity and comorbid mental health risks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that objective digital phenotypes derived from smartphone sensors (e.g., GPS mobility, screen time, communication patterns) and self-reported ecological momentary assessments (EMA) will identify distinct clusters of daily behavior and stress reactivity that predict the severity and functional impact of chronic pain in adolescents.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 adolescents (ages 13-17) with chronic pain and 60 healthy controls. Participants will install a custom research app for a 4-week period, which will passively collect GPS, accelerometer, screen-on/off events, and anonymized call/SMS metadata. Concurrently, brief EMA surveys 3x daily will assess pain intensity, mood, and stress. Data will be processed to extract features like circadian rhythm regularity, location variance, and social activity. We will use unsupervised machine learning (Gaussian Mixture Models) to identify behavioral clusters and supervised models (regularized regression) to test associations between digital phenotypes, salivary cortisol measures (capturing HPA-axis function), and clinical pain outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrative model linking objective digital behavior patterns with stress biology and pain in youth. It will establish proof-of-concept for using smartphone-based digital phenotyping as a low-burden tool for stratifying pediatric pain patients and identifying targets for behavioral intervention. The findings will bridge Dr. Dahl's expertise in adolescent stress, sleep, and affective development with the emerging field of computational psychiatry.
proposals.novelty: While digital phenotyping has been used for depression, its application to chronic pain is novel, particularly in adolescents where pain, stress, and development interact. The integration of passive sensor data with active EMA and neuroendocrine stress markers (cortisol) creates a multi-level, real-world assessment framework not previously employed in pediatric pain research.

proposals.title: Truancy as a Stress-Coping Behavior in Adolescent Suicide: A Post-Mortem Study of TPH2 mRNA Expression in the Amygdala
proposals.motivation: Adolescent suicide is a critical public health issue, yet the functional significance of preceding behaviors like truancy remains poorly understood. Pre-2026 research by Dr. Dahl and others links stress, sleep, and affective disorders to neurobiological dysregulation in youth, particularly in the amygdala and serotonin system. This study directly tests if truancy, a common but often stigmatized behavior, may represent a maladaptive coping strategy with a distinct biological signature in a high-risk population.
proposals.hypothesis: Adolescent suicide victims with a documented history of truancy will exhibit significantly lower TPH2 mRNA expression in the amygdala compared to suicide victims without such a history, indicating a specific neurobiological correlate of a stress-response coping behavior.
proposals.method: We will analyze post-mortem amygdala tissue from a well-characterized adolescent suicide cohort (e.g., from brain banks like the NIMH HBCC). Cases will be stratified into Truancy+ (documented school avoidance) and Truancy- groups, matched for age, sex, and post-mortem interval. TPH2 mRNA levels will be quantified using quantitative real-time PCR (qPCR). Statistical analysis will employ a between-subjects design, using analysis of covariance (ANCOVA) to test for group differences in TPH2 expression, controlling for potential confounders like antidepressant exposure and comorbid depression diagnosis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first molecular evidence linking a specific, complex coping behavior (truancy) to a core neurobiological system (central serotonin synthesis) in adolescent suicide. It will challenge purely pathological views of truancy by framing it within a stress-response model, potentially informing early identification and more nuanced behavioral interventions for at-risk youth.
proposals.novelty: This fusion uniquely bridges sociological concepts of behavioral resistance/coping with precise molecular psychiatry methods in a post-mortem design. It moves beyond correlating psychopathology with biology to instead link a specific *behavioral pattern* with a specific gene expression profile in a defined clinical outcome group.

proposals.title: Serotonergic Polygenic Risk and Heterogeneous Truancy Profiles in Adolescence: A Gene-Environment Interaction Study
proposals.motivation: Truancy is a robust marker of adolescent risk, linked to poor academic, social, and mental health outcomes. Pre-2026 research by Dahl and others highlights the roles of stress reactivity, amygdala function, and serotonin signaling in adolescent emotional and behavioral regulation, yet the genetic architecture underlying specific truancy behaviors remains unexplored. Integrating detailed behavioral phenotyping with genetic risk for serotonin pathway dysfunction can elucidate distinct etiological pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a polygenic risk score (PRS) for serotonin pathway genes (prioritizing TPH2) will be differentially associated with specific, motivationally distinct subtypes of truancy (e.g., internalizing vs. peer-influenced skipping). We further hypothesize that this PRS will moderate the strength of association between known environmental stressors (e.g., family conflict, school climate) and these truancy subtypes.
proposals.method: We will recruit a large, representative cohort of N=2000 adolescents (ages 14-16). Truancy will be measured via a validated survey capturing frequency, social context (alone/with peers), and primary motivation (avoidance, reward-seeking). A serotonin pathway PRS will be calculated using pre-2026 GWAS summary statistics for relevant traits (e.g., neuroticism, amygdala reactivity), focusing on genes like TPH2, SLC6A4, and HTR1A. Primary analyses will use multivariate linear and logistic regression models in R (stats package) to test PRS-behavior associations, followed by moderation analysis (PROCESS macro) to test PRS x environment interactions on specific truancy outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will move beyond treating truancy as a unitary construct by linking serotonergic genetic vulnerability to specific behavioral phenotypes. It will clarify how genetic risk interacts with environmental context to shape divergent school disengagement pathways. The findings could inform targeted, biologically-informed prevention strategies for at-risk youth.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links serotonin to broad externalizing behaviors, this study is novel in applying a pathway-specific PRS to finely-grained, motivationally-defined truancy behaviors. It directly integrates Dahl's prior focus on TPH2, amygdala, and HPA axis stress systems with a developmental psychopathology model of school attendance.

proposals.title: Activity-Pattern Modulation of Serotonin Dynamics in Truant Youth: An Intervention Design Study Integrating Ecological Momentary Assessment and Non-Rate-Limiting TPH2 Models
proposals.motivation: Truancy in adolescence is a significant risk factor for poor mental health, academic failure, and substance use disorders. Pre-2026 research by Dahl and others links these outcomes to stress, sleep disruption, and amygdala reactivity, often implicating serotonin (5-HT) system dysfunction. However, emerging evidence challenges the view that tryptophan hydroxylase-2 (TPH2) is the sole in vivo rate-limiting enzyme for brain 5-HT synthesis, suggesting a more complex, environmentally sensitive regulatory system. This necessitates a shift from static, deficit-based intervention models to dynamic, activity-informed ones.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific patterns of recalled daily activities (e.g., social context, sleep timing, substance exposure) in truant youth will predict distinct profiles of cortisol secretion and amygdala reactivity, which in turn mediate the relationship between those activities and self-reported mood/impulsivity, consistent with activity-driven modulation of alternative 5-HT synthesis pathways rather than a fixed TPH2 deficit.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 truant adolescents (ages 14-17). Using Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) via smartphone, participants will report their activities, social context, and mood multiple times daily for two weeks. This data will be time-synced with actigraphy for sleep/wake cycles. We will employ **between-subject** and **within-subject (time-series)** analyses to classify activity patterns. Participants will then undergo a functional MRI session featuring an amygdala reactivity task (e.g., emotional faces), and provide diurnal cortisol samples. We will use **median** splits of activity pattern clusters to group participants and apply **normal** mixed-effects models to test how activity patterns moderate the links between cortisol, amygdala reactivity, and behavioral measures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, neurobiologically grounded framework for designing targeted behavioral interventions for truancy, moving beyond generic counseling. It will generate empirical data on how real-world activities potentially modulate 5-HT-related neuroendocrine and limbic circuits. The findings could redefine therapeutic targets from correcting a 'chemical imbalance' to strategically modifying specific activity precursors to dysregulation.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely integrates the pre-2026 challenge to the TPH2 rate-limiting paradigm with detailed, real-world activity mapping in a high-risk adolescent population to inform intervention design, a direct fusion of cutting-edge neurobiological theory and clinical developmental psychopathology.

proposals.title: Peer Network Position Modulates the Association Between TPH2 Genotype, Amygdala Reactivity, and Risk-Taking in Truant Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Adolescent risk behaviors like truancy are multiply determined, yet research often isolates neurobiological vulnerabilities (e.g., serotonergic dysfunction linked to TPH2) from potent social contexts. Pre-2026 work by Dahl and others shows that individual differences in amygdala reactivity, partly regulated by TPH2, relate to affective disorders, but the role of the immediate peer environment in moderating this pathway in real-world risk groups is unknown. This gap limits our understanding of how social networks may exacerbate or buffer neurobiological risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adolescent's structural position and influence within a truancy peer network will significantly moderate the relationship between TPH2-linked serotonergic phenotypes (indexed by amygdala reactivity to threat) and the frequency and severity of risk-taking behaviors, such that central network positions attenuate this neurobehavioral association.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 truant adolescents (14-17 years) and map their peer networks using sociometric surveys analyzed with exponential random graph models (ERGMs) to quantify network centrality and influence. Participants will undergo fMRI assessing amygdala reactivity to fearful faces, a well-established probe of serotonergic modulation. We will genotype for the TPH2 rs4570625 regulatory variant and assay peripheral tryptophan levels. Using moderated mediation analysis, we will test if network metrics moderate the path from TPH2 genotype to amygdala reactivity to risk behavior.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated model of how peer network structure interacts with a key neurogenetic pathway to shape adolescent risk. It will move the field beyond main-effect models by identifying specific social-contextual factors that may compensate for or amplify serotonergic vulnerability. The findings could inform targeted, network-aware interventions for high-risk youth.
proposals.novelty: This is novel in its direct fusion of computational social network analysis with neurogenetic and fMRI phenotyping in a naturalistic risk cohort, testing the peer group as an active modulator of a well-defined biological pathway rather than a mere covariate.

proposals.title: Differential Rate Constants of Cortisol Rise and Fall: A Novel Model Linking Stress Reactivity to Adolescent Metabolic and Affective Risk
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Dahl's own work, consistently links altered hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis dynamics to adolescent depression, obesity, and future disorder risk. However, standard models of cortisol reactivity (e.g., area under the curve, single-exponential decay) assume symmetrical rise and fall dynamics, potentially obscuring biologically distinct regulatory processes. This oversimplification limits our ability to pinpoint specific HPA dysregulations that may differentially predict metabolic versus affective outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the rate constant for the rise phase (k_rise) and the rate constant for the fall phase (k_fall) of the cortisol stress response are independently regulated and carry distinct clinical significance. Specifically, a blunted k_rise will be associated with attenuated growth hormone and affective blunting, while a prolonged k_fall (slower recovery) will be linked to metabolic dysregulation and obesity risk in adolescents.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing and newly collected diurnal and stress-induced (TSST) cortisol data from longitudinal cohorts of adolescents with familial risk for affective disorders. Using nonlinear mixed-effects modeling, we will fit a piecewise exponential model to cortisol time-series data, estimating separate k_rise and k_fall parameters for each subject. Model fitting will employ the `nlme` package in R, using restricted maximum likelihood. We will then use multiple regression to test associations between these rate constants and pre-2026 established outcomes: amygdala reactivity (from fMRI), 24-hour GH secretion, adiposity measures, and prospective clinical diagnostic status.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a more nuanced, biologically plausible model of HPA axis dynamics by decoupling reactivity and recovery processes. It will generate novel biomarkers (k_rise, k_fall) that may improve the differentiation of developmental pathways leading to depression versus obesity. The findings could inform more targeted interventions, such as timing-specific stress management strategies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined cortisol magnitude and diurnal slope, explicitly modeling separate kinetic constants for the ascending and descending limbs of the stress response is a novel analytical approach in developmental psychoneuroendocrinology. This moves beyond the standard assumption of a single reactivity/recovery rate.

proposals.title: Validation of a Corticotropin-Releasing Hormone (CRH) Challenge Pharmacokinetic Model in Young Adults with and without a History of Adolescent Depression
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work by Dahl and others has established that hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis dysfunction, measured via CRH challenge tests, is a key biomarker linking adolescent stress to later affective disorders and metabolic risk. However, existing pharmacokinetic models of the HPA axis response to CRH are primarily derived from adolescent cohorts. It is unknown if these models generalize to young adulthood, a critical period for the consolidation or remission of psychopathology, limiting their utility as translational tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a previously validated adolescent CRH challenge pharmacokinetic model will require significant parameter recalibration (e.g., for CRH clearance, pituitary sensitivity) to accurately characterize HPA axis dynamics in young adults, and that model parameters will differentiate those with a history of adolescent depression from healthy controls.
proposals.method: We will recruit 60 young adults (age 25-30): 30 with a history of adolescent major depressive disorder (MDD) in remission and 30 healthy controls. All will undergo a standardized ovine CRH challenge with frequent sampling of ACTH and cortisol. Using pre-2026 methods, we will fit a established two-compartment pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK-PD) model to the data. Model parameters (e.g., CRH elimination rate, ACTH secretion rate constant, cortisol feedback gain) will be estimated using non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM). We will statistically compare parameters between groups and assess model fit using standard metrics (AIC, visual predictive checks).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first validated PK model of CRH challenge response in young adults, extending the developmental framework of HPA axis modeling. It will clarify whether HPA axis dysregulation persists into adulthood as a trait marker following adolescent depression, offering a quantitative tool for predicting long-term clinical and metabolic outcomes.
proposals.novelty: While CRH challenges are common, the formal pharmacokinetic modeling of the response has rarely been applied cross-sectionally across developmental stages. Testing a specific adolescent-derived model in a carefully phenotyped young adult cohort bridges a key gap between developmental psychoneuroendocrinology and quantitative pharmacological modeling.

proposals.title: Functional Data Analysis of Diurnal Cortisol Rhythms: A Novel Approach to Characterizing Stress System Dysregulation in Adolescents with Affective Disorder Risk
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Dahl's work, shows that dysregulated hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis activity, measured via cortisol, is a key biomarker for stress-related disorders in youth. However, traditional analyses (e.g., area-under-the-curve, single time points) fail to capture the full temporal dynamics of the diurnal cortisol rhythm. This limits our ability to identify subtle, clinically meaningful patterns of dysregulation that may predict long-term psychiatric and metabolic outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying functional data analysis (FDA) to intensive longitudinal cortisol data will reveal distinct, temporally-defined patterns of HPA axis dysregulation (e.g., in morning rise, diurnal slope, or evening curvature) that more strongly differentiate adolescents with familial risk for affective disorders from controls, and are more predictive of concurrent amygdala reactivity and future clinical outcomes, than traditional summary metrics.
proposals.method: We will apply FDA techniques to existing and newly collected salivary cortisol time series (e.g., 5-6 samples per day over multiple days). Raw curves will be smoothed using B-spline basis functions. Functional principal component analysis (FPCA) will be used to reduce dimensionality and identify major modes of temporal variation. We will then use functional linear models (FLMs) to regress these functional principal component scores on key outcomes: 1) familial risk status for affective disorders, 2) amygdala reactivity measured via fMRI, and 3) longitudinal clinical and obesity-related outcomes. This will be compared to models using standard summary measures (AUC, slope).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a novel, dynamic characterization of HPA axis function in adolescent development and risk. It will establish FDA as a superior methodological framework for analyzing endocrine time-series data in developmental psychopathology. The findings could identify specific temporal 'signatures' of cortisol secretion that serve as more precise biomarkers for targeted early intervention.
proposals.novelty: While FDA is established in other fields, its application to endocrine data in adolescent psychiatric risk is novel. This moves beyond comparing single time points or summary statistics to model the entire secretory curve as the unit of analysis, capturing shape-based features previously overlooked.

proposals.title: A Dynamical Systems Model of the HPA Axis to Predict Individual Differences in CRH Challenge Responses in Adolescents with Affective Risk
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Dahl's work, has established that hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis dysregulation is a core feature linking adolescent stress, affective disorders, and metabolic outcomes like obesity. However, the field relies heavily on aggregate statistical summaries (e.g., median cortisol levels) from challenge tests, which obscure the underlying mechanistic interactions between CRH, ACTH, and cortisol that drive individual differences in stress reactivity. A more precise, mechanistic model is needed to move beyond descriptive associations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a parsimonious, coupled ordinary differential equation (ODE) model of the HPA axis can accurately simulate individual CRH challenge response curves and that model parameters (e.g., secretion rates, feedback sensitivities) will differentiate adolescents based on familial affective disorder loading and future clinical outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a dynamical systems model using a set of coupled ODEs representing the core HPA axis components (CRH, ACTH, cortisol) with time-delayed feedback. The model will be fit to densely sampled ACTH and cortisol time-series data from existing CRH challenge studies in adolescents. We will employ a Bayesian parameter estimation framework (e.g., using Stan or similar MCMC algorithms) to infer individual-specific model parameters. These parameters will then be used as features in regression models to test associations with familial risk, amygdala reactivity (from fMRI), and longitudinal clinical outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for interpreting neuroendocrine challenge tests, shifting from summary statistics to mechanistic parameters. It will directly test how specific components of HPA axis dynamics (e.g., feedback gain) contribute to the attenuated growth hormone and cortisol patterns observed in at-risk youth, offering new targets for early intervention.
proposals.novelty: While ODE models exist in theoretical endocrinology, applying them to fit empirical, individual-level challenge data in a developmental psychopathology context is novel. This approach moves beyond using 'time' as a simple covariate in linear models to explicitly modeling the temporal dynamics and feedback loops that define the system's behavior.

proposals.title: Validating a Salivary Cortisol-Based Model of Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis Dynamics for Pediatric Stress and Obesity Research
proposals.motivation: Invasive blood sampling for cortisol measurement is a significant barrier to studying stress physiology, particularly in pediatric populations where repeated measures are crucial for understanding development. Salivary cortisol offers a non-invasive alternative, but existing models of HPA axis function (e.g., from Dahl's prior work) are built on plasma data, and their parameters may not translate directly to salivary measures due to known differences in assay dynamics and diurnal rhythms. Adapting these models for saliva is essential to enable larger-scale, ecologically valid studies of stress in children at risk for affective disorders and obesity.
proposals.hypothesis: A mathematical model of HPA axis dynamics, originally parameterized for plasma cortisol, can be successfully adapted and validated for salivary cortisol data, preserving its ability to differentiate key regulatory features (e.g., circadian amplitude, feedback sensitivity) relevant to pediatric stress and metabolic risk.
proposals.method: We will adapt a previously published differential equation model of HPA axis regulation (informed by Dahl's work on 24h cortisol secretion). Using existing paired plasma and salivary cortisol time-series data from pediatric cohorts (e.g., from studies of depression or obesity risk), we will employ non-linear mixed-effects modeling to estimate population and individual parameters for the salivary data. Key steps include: 1) defining a transfer function between plasma and salivary cortisol compartments, 2) using maximum likelihood estimation (e.g., via the `nlme` package in R) to fit the model, and 3) validating the model by testing if derived parameters (like cortisol awakening response magnitude and diurnal slope) predict clinical outcomes (e.g., depressive symptoms, BMI) as effectively as plasma-based parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a validated, open-source computational tool for analyzing salivary cortisol rhythms in children, directly reducing participant burden. It will establish the psychometric validity of model-derived HPA axis parameters from saliva, facilitating more widespread investigation of stress mechanisms in developmental psychopathology and pediatric obesity research.
proposals.novelty: While salivary cortisol is commonly used, the novel adaptation of a formal physiological regulatory model (vs. simple summary statistics) to salivary data represents a methodological advance, allowing for the inference of latent system properties from a less invasive biomarker in pediatric populations.

proposals.title: Inferring Endogenous CRH Tone from Cortisol Dynamics: A Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic Modeling Approach in Adolescents with Affective Risk
proposals.motivation: Corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) is a central regulator of the HPA axis, and its dysregulation is implicated in stress-related disorders, obesity, and depression. However, directly measuring endogenous CRH tone in humans is invasive and difficult. A method to infer CRH activity from the more readily measurable cortisol curve would provide a crucial, non-invasive window into central stress system function, particularly for developmental studies in at-risk youth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a validated pharmacokinetic-pharmacodynamic (PK-PD) model of the HPA axis can be inverted to provide a robust, quantitative estimate of the effective CRH stimulus (exogenous dose or endogenous tone) from an individual's cortisol response profile.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-stage approach. First, we will construct a forward PK-PD model integrating known physiology: CRH-stimulated ACTH release, ACTH-driven cortisol synthesis, and cortisol negative feedback. Model parameters will be informed by pre-2026 CRH challenge studies. Second, we will treat the inverse problem—estimating CRH input from cortisol output—as an optimization task. We will employ a Bayesian framework, using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to infer the most probable CRH dose/time-course given an observed cortisol curve, accounting for individual variability in model parameters (e.g., glandular sensitivity, clearance rates).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational tool for non-invasive estimation of central CRH drive. It will advance the mechanistic understanding of HPA axis dysregulation in adolescent psychopathology by allowing retrospective analysis of existing cortisol datasets (e.g., diurnal, stress-induced) in terms of inferred CRH activity. This bridges a critical gap between peripheral endocrine measures and central neuroendocrine processes.
proposals.novelty: While forward models of HPA axis dynamics exist, the explicit inversion of such a model to infer CRH input from cortisol output is a novel application. The integration of a Bayesian inference framework to solve this inverse problem for individual subjects, moving beyond group-level correlations, represents a significant methodological advance in developmental psychoneuroendocrinology.

proposals.title: Pharmacokinetic Modeling of ACTH Response to CRH Challenge in Adolescents: Linking HPA Axis Dynamics to Familial Risk for Affective Disorders
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dahl and others has established that hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis dysfunction, particularly in response to corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) challenge, is a key biomarker in adolescents at familial risk for affective disorders. However, prior analyses of CRH challenge data often rely on summary measures (e.g., area under the curve, peak response), which fail to capture the dynamic temporal profile of adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) secretion. A detailed pharmacokinetic model of the ACTH response is needed to quantify the pituitary's sensitivity and reactivity to CRH, a critical intermediate step in the stress cascade linking neural regulation to cortisol output.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a two-compartment pharmacokinetic model will reveal distinct parameter clusters (e.g., CRH clearance rate, ACTH secretion amplitude) that differentiate adolescents with familial loading for affective disorder from controls, and that these model-derived parameters will show stronger associations with concurrent psychiatric symptoms and future clinical outcomes than traditional summary measures.
proposals.method: We will apply pharmacokinetic modeling to existing ACTH time-series data from CRH challenge studies in adolescents. The core approach will use non-linear mixed-effects modeling (NONMEM) to fit a two-compartment model, where the first compartment represents CRH distribution/clearance and drives ACTH secretion in the second compartment. Model parameters (e.g., elimination rate constant k_e, secretion proportionality constant S) will be estimated for each subject using maximum likelihood estimation. We will then use median-based robust regression and between-group comparisons (high-risk vs. control) to test for parameter differences, controlling for covariates like age and sex.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for characterizing individual differences in pituitary gland dynamics within the HPA axis. It will advance the field beyond static hormonal measures by offering a mechanistic model of a key stress response component. The resulting parameters could serve as more precise endophenotypes for genetic studies and risk stratification in developmental psychopathology.
proposals.novelty: While pharmacokinetic modeling has been applied to cortisol data, its application to the preceding ACTH response in a developmental, high-risk context is novel. This approach moves from describing group-level hormone curves to estimating individual-specific physiological parameters that reflect underlying pituitary function.

proposals.title: A Unified Mathematical Model of Cortisol Dynamics Across Standardized Psychosocial Stressors in Adolescents
proposals.motivation: The Trier Social Stress Test (TSST) is a gold-standard psychosocial stressor, but its cortisol response is often analyzed in isolation. Pre-2026 research by Dr. Dahl and others shows that individual differences in HPA-axis reactivity to stress are central to risk for affective disorders and obesity in youth. A model that can characterize cortisol trajectories across different validated stressors (e.g., TSST, socially evaluated cold pressor, public speaking) would allow direct comparison of stress system function under varied challenges, moving beyond single-test paradigms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a single, parsimonious non-linear mixed-effects model (e.g., a piecewise or logistic growth model) can accurately capture the rise and recovery of salivary cortisol across multiple laboratory psychosocial stressors, and that model parameters (e.g., peak amplitude, recovery slope) will show stronger associations with clinical risk factors (e.g., familial affective disorder, obesity) than traditional summary metrics like area under the curve.
proposals.method: We will apply the same model form to existing salivary cortisol data from adolescents who completed the TSST and at least one other validated stressor. Using a Bayesian hierarchical framework (e.g., Stan), we will fit models with subject-level random effects for key parameters. Model selection will compare fits of exponential rise-and-decay, logistic, and piecewise linear forms using Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion (WAIC). The primary analysis will test if the between-subject variance of recovery time and peak reactivity parameters is consistent across stressors, and if these parameters correlate with pre-existing measures of amygdala reactivity (from fMRI) and 24-hour cortisol secretion.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative tool for characterizing an individual's stress response profile across contexts, enhancing the precision of HPA-axis phenotyping. It will directly inform developmental models of stress vulnerability by linking dynamic model parameters to neural and clinical risk markers, bridging Dr. Dahl's work on cortisol, amygdala function, and adolescent psychopathology.
proposals.novelty: While cortisol responses to single stressors are commonly modeled, applying a consistent formal model across different psychosocial stressors to derive generalizable, individual-specific parameters of reactivity and regulation is a novel integrative approach. This moves beyond comparing mean group responses to modeling the individual's stress response system itself.

proposals.title: Sleep Architecture as a Biomarker of Metabolic Health: Testing the Normative Benchmark of Normal-Weight Children
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research establishes strong bidirectional links between poor sleep and obesity in youth, but often treats obesity as the primary pathology. This approach implicitly assumes the sleep of normal-weight children is the healthy standard. However, given the complex interplay of stress, HPA axis function, and neural circuits (e.g., amygdala reactivity) influencing both sleep and weight, it is critical to empirically test if normal-weight sleep patterns are indeed optimal for all children, including those with overweight/obesity or other metabolic risks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that sleep architecture (e.g., slow-wave sleep, REM latency, sleep continuity) in normal-weight children represents a distinct, optimal profile for metabolic and neuroendocrine health, and that deviations from this profile in children with overweight/obesity will correlate with dysregulated cortisol secretion and altered amygdala reactivity to affective stimuli.
proposals.method: We will recruit 180 adolescents (ages 12-16), stratified into normal-weight (BMI 5th-85th percentile), overweight (85th-95th), and obese (>95th) groups. All participants will undergo 7-night actigraphy and 2-night in-lab polysomnography to quantify sleep architecture. We will measure 24-hour cortisol secretion profiles and assess amygdala reactivity using fMRI during an emotional face-matching task. Statistical analyses will use mixed-effects models to compare sleep parameters between groups (normal, median, between). We will then employ mediation and moderation analyses to test if sleep architecture mediates the relationship between weight status and neuroendocrine/stress reactivity outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This study will move the field from association to mechanism by testing a foundational assumption. It will clarify whether sleep interventions for pediatric obesity should aim to normalize sleep to a weight-specific benchmark or to a universal, healthy-weight standard. The findings will also inform whether sleep architecture serves as an early biomarker for metabolic dysregulation linked to stress system function.
proposals.novelty: This proposal directly challenges the implicit normative standard in pediatric sleep-obesity research. It uniquely integrates Dahl's expertise in sleep, stress (HPA axis), and affective neuroscience (amygdala) to test if the 'optimal' sleep phenotype is weight-dependent, moving beyond simple comparisons of sleep duration.

proposals.title: The Metabolic Paradox of Sleep in Pediatric Obesity: Investigating the Correlates of Longer Sleep Duration
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research shows that short sleep duration is a risk factor for pediatric obesity. However, some studies find that children with obesity sleep longer than their lean peers, a counterintuitive finding. It is critical to determine if this longer sleep is a protective, compensatory response to metabolic dysregulation or a marker of a different pathological process, such as sleep-disordered breathing or depression, which are also linked to obesity and altered HPA axis function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that longer sleep duration in adolescents with obesity, compared to lean controls, will be associated with negative metabolic and neuroendocrine correlates, including higher insulin resistance, dysregulated cortisol secretion, and altered amygdala reactivity to affective stimuli.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 adolescents (ages 12-16), stratified by BMI percentile (obese/lean) and median sleep duration (actigraphy over 7 days). Primary outcomes are HOMA-IR (fasting glucose/insulin) and 24-hour salivary cortisol secretion profiles (sampled 8x/day). A subsample (n=60) will undergo fMRI to assess amygdala reactivity using an established emotional faces task. We will use between-subjects ANCOVA to test for group (obesity status) by sleep duration (high/low) interactions on metabolic and cortisol outcomes, controlling for age, sex, and puberty. For fMRI, a 2x2 factorial design will model amygdala BOLD response, with post-hoc tests on extracted parameter estimates.
proposals.contribution: This study will clarify the clinical meaning of extended sleep in youth with obesity, moving beyond simple duration metrics. It will directly test whether longer sleep co-occurs with adverse metabolic and stress physiology, informing targeted interventions. By integrating metabolic, HPA axis, and neural reactivity measures, it will offer a multi-system perspective on sleep-obesity mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: While most research focuses on sleep deficiency as a risk factor, this proposal uniquely investigates the potential pathological significance of longer sleep in an at-risk population. It integrates Dahl's expertise in stress neurobiology (cortisol, amygdala) with sleep and obesity research in a novel, mechanistic way.

proposals.title: Re-evaluating the Clinical Significance of Pediatric Obstructive Sleep Apnea: A Multi-Modal Investigation of AHI Thresholds in Children with Obesity
proposals.motivation: The pediatric apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) threshold of ≥5 events/hour for diagnosing obstructive sleep apnea (OSA) is widely used but may not be appropriate for children with obesity, a population with distinct pathophysiology. Pre-2026 evidence suggests children with obesity exhibit significant metabolic and neurocognitive morbidity even at lower AHI levels, yet clinical practice relies on generic thresholds, potentially delaying intervention and contributing to adverse outcomes like insulin resistance and affective dysregulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in children with obesity, adverse cardiometabolic and neuroendocrine outcomes (e.g., insulin resistance, cortisol dysregulation) and altered amygdala reactivity will be detectable at AHI levels below the standard clinical threshold of 5, indicating a need for a lower, obesity-specific AHI cutoff for clinical significance.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing polysomnography, neuroimaging, and endocrine data from a longitudinal cohort of children (ages 9-16) with obesity, stratified by AHI (e.g., <1, 1-2.9, 3-4.9, ≥5). Primary analyses will use mixed-effects models to test for **between**-group differences in outcomes (24-hour cortisol secretion via area-under-the-curve, amygdala reactivity to emotional faces via fMRI). We will determine the optimal AHI threshold for predicting morbidity using receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve analysis and **median** split comparisons of high vs. low morbidity groups, controlling for adiposity and other confounds.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated evidence linking sub-threshold OSA in pediatric obesity to measurable neuroendocrine and neural stress system dysfunction, directly informing clinical guidelines. It will propose a data-driven, lower AHI threshold for triggering clinical intervention in this high-risk population, potentially improving early detection and prevention of obesity-related comorbidities.
proposals.novelty: While AHI thresholds have been questioned, no pre-2026 study has explicitly tested for obesity-specific thresholds by integrating polysomnographic data with the precise neuroendocrine and amygdala reactivity measures central to Dr. Dahl's research on stress and affective risk in youth.

proposals.title: Sleep-Stage-Specific Metabolic Rate and Its Association with Amygdala Reactivity and Stress Hormones in Adolescents with Obesity Risk
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dahl and others has established that sleep disruption is a key pathway linking childhood stress to obesity and affective disorders. However, the real-time metabolic physiology of different sleep stages in youth remains poorly characterized, creating a critical gap in understanding the mechanistic bridge between neural stress reactivity (e.g., amygdala), HPA axis function, and energy balance dysregulation during a sensitive developmental period.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents with familial risk for affective disorders and obesity will exhibit a blunted reduction in metabolic rate during slow-wave sleep (SWS), and that this attenuated metabolic dip will be correlated with heightened amygdala reactivity to threat and altered overnight cortisol secretion.
proposals.method: We will combine overnight polysomnography (PSG) with a ventilated hood indirect calorimetry system to measure minute-by-minute energy expenditure (EEG) and respiratory quotient (RQ). Sleep stages (N1, N2, SWS, REM) will be scored using standard criteria. The primary analysis will use mixed-effects models to compare EE between stages and groups. We will integrate these data with pre-sleep fMRI amygdala reactivity tasks (using methods from Dahl's prior TPH2 gene work) and serial salivary cortisol measures, employing mediation analysis to test if sleep-stage metabolism explains links between neural stress response and metabolic risk.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-temporal-resolution map of energy expenditure across the sleep architecture in at-risk adolescents. It will directly test a novel physiological mechanism—sleep-stage-specific metabolic dysregulation—linking Dahl's established lines of research on neural stress circuits, HPA axis function, and obesity. Findings could identify a new biomarker for targeted interventions.
proposals.novelty: While PSG and indirect calorimetry exist separately, their synchronized application to dissect sleep-stage metabolism in a developmentally sensitive, at-risk population is novel. Integrating this metabolic signal with amygdala reactivity and cortisol measures creates a unique multi-system model not previously tested.
