proposals.title: Real-Time Dynamics of Daytime Sleepiness, Stress, and Reward-Seeking Behavior in Children: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Study
proposals.motivation: Childhood sleep problems are robustly linked to stress dysregulation, obesity, and affective disorders, but the real-time, within-day mechanisms remain poorly understood. Pre-2026 research by Dahl and others has established links between sleep architecture, HPA axis function, and amygdala reactivity, yet these studies typically rely on retrospective or laboratory measures that fail to capture dynamic, ecologically valid processes. Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) offers a powerful method to bridge this gap by capturing momentary states in naturalistic settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that real-time fluctuations in daytime sleepiness, as measured by EMA, will predict subsequent increases in momentary stress appraisals and unhealthy, reward-driven snacking behaviors in children, and that these associations will be stronger in children with higher baseline amygdala reactivity or familial risk for affective disorders.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 children (ages 10-12) oversampled for familial risk of affective disorder. Participants will complete a 7-day EMA protocol via smartphone, with 5 random prompts per day assessing sleepiness (visual analog scale), stress, affect, and context. They will also log all food/beverage intake. Prompts will use signal-contingent and event-contingent sampling. Data will be analyzed using multilevel modeling to examine within-person, time-lagged associations (e.g., morning sleepiness predicting afternoon behavior). We will integrate pre-study fMRI amygdala reactivity data (from an emotional faces task) and diurnal cortisol profiles as moderators of these EMA-derived pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first fine-grained, real-time characterization of how sleepiness propagates through the day to influence stress and health-relevant behaviors in children's natural environments. It will directly extend Dahl's prior work on HPA axis dysfunction and affective risk by testing these mechanisms dynamically. Findings could inform just-in-time adaptive interventions targeting sleepiness to prevent stress-related and obesity-promoting behaviors.
proposals.novelty: While EMA has been used in adolescent mood research, its application to capture *daytime sleepiness* as a dynamic predictor of stress and eating behavior in children is novel. The integration of real-time EMA data with established neural (amygdala reactivity) and endocrine (cortisol) biomarkers of vulnerability represents a significant methodological advance over static, between-person studies.

proposals.title: Experimental Manipulation of Slow-Wave Sleep and Its Impact on Next-Day Food Reward Processing and Ad Libitum Intake in Children at Risk for Obesity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research establishes that short sleep duration in children is a risk factor for obesity, but the causal mechanisms linking specific sleep architecture features to eating behavior remain poorly understood. This gap is critical because childhood obesity is a major public health concern, and sleep may be a modifiable intervention target. Understanding the neurobiological pathway—particularly how sleep modulates amygdala-centered reward circuits involved in food choice—is a logical next step from Dr. Dahl's prior work on stress, HPA axis, and neural reactivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an experimental reduction in slow-wave sleep (SWS) in children will lead to (1) increased amygdala reactivity to high-calorie food cues the following day, and (2) greater ad libitum intake of palatable, energy-dense foods, with these effects being more pronounced in children with familial risk for obesity.
proposals.method: We will employ a within-subjects, counterbalanced crossover design with two in-lab conditions: SWS disruption via targeted acoustic stimulation (using real-time EEG to detect slow oscillations) and an undisturbed sleep control. Polysomnography will quantify sleep architecture (SWS, REM, latency). The next day, participants will undergo fMRI while performing a food reward valuation task, with a focus on amygdala and prefrontal reactivity. Ad libitum food intake will be measured from a standardized, multi-item buffet lunch. Data analysis will use linear mixed-effects models to test the 'between' condition effects on neural and behavioral outcomes, 'median' splits for risk stratification, and 'time' (circadian) covariates.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first experimental evidence in children causally linking a specific sleep neurophysiology (SWS) to food-related neural circuitry and real-world eating behavior. It will identify a potential neurobiological mechanism (altered amygdala reactivity) for the sleep-obesity link. The findings could inform targeted, sleep-focused prevention strategies for children at high risk for obesity.
proposals.novelty: While observational studies link sleep and diet, this proposal uniquely uses an experimental 'inverse problem' approach to manipulate a precise neural sleep feature (SWS) and measure its downstream effects on brain and behavior, moving beyond simple duration measures. It integrates Dr. Dahl's expertise in developmental affective neuroscience (amygdala reactivity) with experimental sleep science to test a mechanistic pathway.

proposals.title: Structural and Functional Amygdala-Hypothalamic-Pituitary Circuitry as a Neuroimaging Biomarker for Suicidal Thoughts in Adolescents: An ENIGMA-Suicidal Thoughts and Behavior (ENIGMA-STB) Consortium Study
proposals.motivation: Suicidal thoughts and behavior (STB) in adolescents represent a critical public health crisis, yet their neurobiological underpinnings remain poorly understood, hindering objective risk assessment. Pre-2026 research by Dr. Dahl and others has established links between stress system dysregulation (HPA-axis), altered amygdala reactivity, and affective disorders in youth, but these findings have not been systematically integrated and tested as biomarkers for STB across large, diverse cohorts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents with STB will exhibit distinct, reproducible neuroimaging signatures, specifically characterized by altered amygdala structure and functional connectivity to hypothalamic and prefrontal regions, and that these neural correlates will be moderated by HPA-axis function (e.g., cortisol secretion) and genetic variants influencing serotonin pathways (e.g., TPH2).
proposals.method: We will employ the ENIGMA meta-analysis framework to harmonize structural (T1-weighted) and resting-state functional MRI data from international cohorts of adolescents with and without STB. Primary analyses will use standardized protocols for subcortical volumetry (e.g., amygdala) and seed-based functional connectivity (amygdala as seed). We will apply mixed-effects models to test for case-control differences, covarying for age, sex, and scanner. Secondary analyses will integrate hormonal (24h cortisol) and genetic (TPH2 variant) data where available, using mediation and moderation models to explore mechanistic pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish the first large-scale, pre-registered neuroimaging biomarkers for adolescent STB, directly translating Dr. Dahl's prior work on stress, amygdala, and development to a high-risk clinical phenotype. It will create a foundational dataset for the ENIGMA-STB working group and identify potential neural targets for future intervention studies aimed at mitigating suicide risk.
proposals.novelty: This proposal uniquely synthesizes Dr. Dahl's expertise in developmental stress physiology with the power of ENIGMA's consortium neuroscience, moving beyond disorder-level diagnoses (e.g., depression) to pinpoint transdiagnostic neural circuitry specific to suicidal ideation and behavior in the adolescent brain.

proposals.title: Harmonizing Real-World Behavioral and Cognitive Phenotypes Across Pediatric Disorders: A Consortium Approach to Uncover Transdiagnostic Pathways
proposals.motivation: Current neurodevelopmental and psychiatric research, including large-scale consortia, often relies on static brain structure and categorical diagnoses, which obscure the rich, dynamic behavioral and cognitive dysfunction that cuts across disorders and impacts daily life. Pre-2026, there is a critical need to move beyond these siloed, brain-centric models to understand how shared behavioral phenotypes (e.g., sleep dysregulation, stress reactivity, executive function deficits) underlie co-morbidities like obesity and affective disorders in youth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that harmonizing high-dimensional, real-world behavioral, cognitive, and daily functioning data across diagnostic groups will reveal distinct, clinically meaningful transdiagnostic phenotypes that are more strongly associated with underlying neuroendocrine (e.g., HPA axis) and neural (e.g., amygdala reactivity) mechanisms than traditional diagnostic categories.
proposals.method: We will expand an existing neuroimaging consortium framework to pool and harmonize deep phenotyping data from multiple longitudinal cohorts of children and adolescents. Data will include actigraphy-derived sleep patterns, ecological momentary assessment (EMA) of mood and stress, cognitive battery scores, and daily functioning metrics. We will apply data-driven techniques, including k-means clustering and latent profile analysis, to identify behavioral/cognitive subtypes. These subtypes will then be tested for associations with pre-harmonized consortium neuroimaging (amygdala reactivity) and neuroendocrine (diurnal cortisol, growth hormone) data using mixed-effects models, controlling for age, sex, and puberty.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, publicly available harmonized dataset of real-world behavioral phenotypes for the pediatric research community. It will advance the field by establishing concrete methods for transdiagnostic data integration and by identifying specific, measurable behavioral pathways that link neural and endocrine dysfunction to clinical outcomes across disorders.
proposals.novelty: While consortia have successfully pooled neuroimaging data, the systematic harmonization of intensive, real-world behavioral and cognitive measures across disorders in youth is novel. This approach directly tests whether daily-life functioning provides a more powerful lens for understanding neurobiological mechanisms than diagnosis or brain structure alone.

proposals.title: Social and Metabolic Cues Override the Inverted Light-Dark Cycle: A Study of Circadian Melatonin Resynchronization in Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, the light-dark cycle is established as the primary zeitgeber for the human circadian system. However, modern life involves frequent circadian disruptions (e.g., shift work, screen time), particularly in adolescents, which are linked to mood disorders and metabolic risk. It is unknown whether robust, competing non-photic cues can functionally override an inverted light schedule to maintain normative hormonal rhythms, a question critical for developing interventions for at-risk youth.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a consistent, potent regimen of scheduled social interaction, physical activity, and caloric intake will override an inverted 12:12 light-dark cycle, leading to a resynchronization of the dim-light melatonin onset (DLMO) rhythm to the non-photic schedule rather than the photic schedule.
proposals.method: We will recruit 60 healthy adolescents (14-16 years). In a 2-week laboratory protocol, the light-dark cycle will be inverted (lights on 2200-1000h). Participants will be randomized to a control group (minimal non-photic structure) or an experimental group receiving a strict schedule of social tasks, moderate exercise, and meal timing aligned with a normal day (0800-2000h). Salivary melatonin will be sampled hourly over 24-hour periods at baseline, midpoint, and endpoint. Primary analysis will use mixed-effects models to compare the timing of DLMO between groups, with time (day) and group (control vs. experimental) as fixed effects and subject as a random effect, testing for a significant group-by-time interaction.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct experimental evidence in humans for the upper limits of non-photic entrainment strength against a conflicting photic driver. It will clarify the potential for behavioral interventions to mitigate circadian misalignment. The findings will directly inform strategies for populations like adolescents with delayed sleep phase or those in forced shift-work conditions.
proposals.novelty: While non-photic cues are known to modulate circadian rhythms, no pre-2026 study has tested if a curated suite of such cues can actively dominate and resynchronize a core rhythm like melatonin in the face of a directly opposing, high-intensity light-dark cycle.

proposals.title: Melatonin as a Mediator: Testing the Hypothesis that Altered Circadian Hormone Secretion in Adolescent Depression is a Consequence of Stress-Related Amygdala-HPA Axis Dysfunction
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research consistently links altered melatonin rhythms to adolescent depression and its associated risks for obesity and poor outcomes, but the causal direction remains unclear. This is a critical gap because if melatonin dysfunction is merely an epiphenomenon of upstream pathophysiology, targeting it directly may be ineffective. Clarifying this is essential for developing precise, mechanism-based interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that altered dim-light melatonin onset (DLMO) and amplitude in depressed adolescents are not primary causal factors but are secondary consequences of stress-sensitive neural circuitry dysfunction, specifically heightened amygdala reactivity and subsequent HPA axis dysregulation.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 adolescents (ages 12-16): 60 with current MDD and 60 healthy controls. We will measure: 1) amygdala reactivity to fearful faces via fMRI, 2) 24-hour cortisol secretion profiles via salivary sampling, and 3) DLMO and melatonin amplitude via serial salivary sampling. Using structural equation modeling (SEM), we will test a mediation model where diagnostic status predicts amygdala reactivity, which predicts cortisol dysregulation (attenuated growth hormone response or elevated evening cortisol), which in turn predicts melatonin parameters. We will control for sleep timing and pubertal stage.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated neural-endocrine model positioning melatonin within the known stress-pathophysiology of depression. It will clarify whether melatonin is a viable treatment target or a biomarker of more central amygdala-HPA axis disturbance. The findings will inform the development of stratified interventions for depressed adolescents based on their underlying neurobiological profile.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has examined these systems in isolation, this proposal uniquely tests a specific, directional mediational pathway from amygdala to HPA axis to pineal output, directly challenging the assumption that melatonin alterations are a primary etiological factor in adolescent depression.

proposals.title: Truancy as Circadian Self-Preservation: Linking Peripheral Melatonin Dysregulation, Sensory Sensitivity, and School Avoidance in Adolescent Depression
proposals.motivation: Adolescent depression is a major public health concern, yet its behavioral manifestations like truancy are often framed as purely volitional or oppositional, leading to punitive responses. Pre-2026 research by our group and others has established strong links between adolescent depression, stress system dysregulation (HPA axis), sleep/circadian disruption, and altered amygdala reactivity. Simultaneously, emerging evidence indicates melatonin is synthesized in peripheral tissues (retina, gut, skin) and may influence local circadian timing and sensory processing, but its role in adolescent psychopathology is unexplored. This study bridges these areas to investigate truancy as a potential biologically-influenced coping behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a subgroup of depressed adolescents who engage in truancy will exhibit greater dysregulation of peripheral circadian markers (specifically, altered diurnal profiles of salivary melatonin and its metabolites) and heightened subjective light sensitivity compared to non-truant depressed peers, and that these physiological measures will correlate with self-reported aversive reactions to the school environment.
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 adolescents (ages 14-17) diagnosed with Major Depressive Disorder, stratified into high-truancy (≥15% days absent) and low-truancy (<5%) groups. We will collect serial saliva samples across 24 hours to assay melatonin and 6-sulfatoxymelatonin, quantifying rhythm parameters (mesor, amplitude, acrophase) using cosinor analysis. Participants will complete validated questionnaires on light sensitivity, GI symptoms, and school-related distress. A nested qualitative sub-study (n=30) will use semi-structured interviews to explore the subjective experience of truancy. We will use multivariate analysis of covariance (MANCOVA) to test group differences in circadian and sensory measures, controlling for depression severity, and mediation analysis to test if sensory sensitivity explains the link between circadian markers and truancy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical test of a novel biobehavioral model framing truancy as a coping response to circadian-driven sensory dysregulation. It will generate new data on peripheral melatonin rhythms in adolescent depression and their relationship to real-world behavior. By integrating quantitative biomarkers with qualitative lived experience, the findings could shift clinical and educational perspectives on school avoidance from a disciplinary issue to a potential target for chronobiological and sensory interventions.
proposals.novelty: This proposal is novel in directly connecting the emerging biology of extra-pineal melatonin to a complex adolescent behavior (truancy) and in its explicit framing of a 'problem behavior' as a functional coping strategy, integrating molecular psychiatry with a critical, person-centered perspective on psychopathology.

proposals.title: A Pragmatic Trial of Melatonergic Agonists for Adolescent Depression: Effects on Mood, Sleep, and Amygdala-HPA Axis Function in Diverse Clinical Settings
proposals.motivation: Adolescent depression is a major public health concern, often comorbid with sleep and circadian disruption and linked to dysregulated stress systems (HPA axis) and amygdala reactivity. While melatonergic agonists (e.g., ramelteon, agomelatine) show promise for mood regulation by targeting circadian rhythms, their real-world effectiveness in heterogeneous adolescent populations, and their impact on underlying neural and endocrine pathways, remains poorly characterized.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adjunctive treatment with a melatonergic agonist, compared to treatment-as-usual, will lead to greater improvements in depressive symptoms in clinically referred adolescents, mediated by improvements in objectively measured sleep and associated with normalization of amygdala reactivity to threat and 24-hour cortisol secretion patterns.
proposals.method: We will conduct a pragmatic, randomized controlled trial across diverse pediatric and mental health clinics. Adolescents (n=200, ages 13-17) with Major Depressive Disorder will be randomized to receive a melatonergic agonist (ramelteon) or placebo adjunctive to standard care. Primary outcomes are clinician-rated depression scores over 12 weeks. Key mechanistic measures include: 1) wrist-actigraphy for sleep midpoint and regularity (analyzed using median and between-person comparisons), 2) diurnal salivary cortisol (area-under-the-curve and slope), and 3) fMRI amygdala reactivity to fearful faces. We will use multilevel modeling to assess time-by-group interactions and mediation analyses to test sleep and neurobiological pathways.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide critical evidence on the real-world clinical utility of circadian-targeted interventions for adolescent depression. It will uniquely elucidate whether therapeutic effects are tied to measurable improvements in sleep physiology and concomitant changes in stress (HPA) and threat-processing (amygdala) systems, bridging clinical trials with neurodevelopmental mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: This integrates a pragmatic effectiveness design with deep phenotyping of sleep and stress biology, directly testing a circadian mechanism of action in a high-risk developmental period. It moves beyond efficacy in controlled settings to assess function in diverse, real-world clinical practice.

proposals.title: Gut Microbiome Composition and Gastrointestinal Melatonin Synthesis in Adolescents with Depression: A Multi-Omic Investigation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dr. Dahl and others has established that adolescent depression involves complex interactions between stress physiology (HPA axis), sleep disruption, and neural circuits like the amygdala. Emerging evidence suggests the gut microbiome influences mood and behavior, potentially via modulating neuroactive compounds. Gastrointestinal (GI) tissues produce over 400 times more melatonin than the pineal gland, yet the role of this peripheral melatonin and its interaction with gut microbes in adolescent depression remains unexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents with depression will exhibit distinct gut microbiome profiles associated with altered levels of GI-derived melatonin and its metabolites, and that these microbial-melatonin interactions will correlate with clinical severity and established neuroendocrine stress markers (e.g., cortisol).
proposals.method: We will recruit 120 adolescents (60 with Major Depressive Disorder, 60 healthy controls), aged 12-17, from existing longitudinal cohorts. Fecal samples will undergo shotgun metagenomic sequencing for comprehensive taxonomic and functional profiling. Plasma and fecal levels of melatonin and its primary metabolite, 6-sulfatoxymelatonin, will be quantified by LC-MS/MS. We will apply mediation analysis to test if microbiome features (e.g., relative abundance of melatonin-metabolizing bacteria like *Clostridium* spp.) mediate the relationship between depression status and melatonin levels. Statistical models will control for sleep (actigraphy), diet, and stress (diurnal cortisol), using repeated-measures designs where possible.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first integrated analysis of the gut microbiome and the GI melatonin system in adolescent depression. It will identify specific microbial taxa and pathways linked to peripheral melatonin signaling, offering novel mechanistic targets. The findings could bridge the gap between gut dysbiosis, sleep-related complaints, and stress pathophysiology in depression, informing future microbiome-targeted interventions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has separately examined the microbiome in depression and the role of pineal melatonin in sleep, the investigation of GI-derived melatonin as a potential microbial interface in adolescent psychopathology is novel. Integrating this with Dr. Dahl's expertise in developmental stress physiology and deep phenotyping moves beyond correlation to test mechanistic pathways.

proposals.title: Actigraphy-Derived Circadian Phase and Amplitude as a Correlate of Melatonin Secretion in Children with Major Depressive Disorder
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dr. Dahl and others has established that circadian rhythm disruptions are a core feature of adolescent depression and a risk factor for metabolic disorders like obesity. However, assessing circadian phase in children typically requires intensive methods like serial melatonin measurement. A validated, continuous, and non-invasive actigraphy-based measure of circadian timing would be a major advance for developmental psychopathology research and clinical screening.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific actigraphy-derived metrics of circadian rest-activity rhythms (particularly the timing of the least active 5-hour period, L5) will show a strong temporal correlation with the onset of urinary 6-sulfatoxymelatonin (6-SMT) excretion in depressed children, and that the amplitude of these rhythms will be attenuated compared to healthy controls.
proposals.method: We will recruit 60 children (8-12 years) with MDD and 40 healthy controls. Participants will wear a wrist actigraph (Actiwatch Spectrum) for 7 consecutive days and nights while collecting all urine for 6-SMT assay. Actigraphy data will be processed using Cole-Kripke sleep-wake algorithms and analyzed with non-parametric circadian rhythm analysis (NPCRA) to derive interdaily stability, intradaily variability, relative amplitude, and L5 timing. The primary analysis will use cross-correlation functions to assess the time-lagged relationship between the L5 midpoint and the evening rise in 6-SMT excretion. Secondary analyses will use linear mixed-effects models to compare rhythm metrics between groups, controlling for age, sex, and BMI.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a validated, objective method for estimating circadian phase in children using actigraphy, directly linking motor activity to a gold-standard neuroendocrine marker. It will characterize the specific nature of circadian disruption in childhood depression, informing future interventions targeting sleep-wake cycles. The findings could establish actigraphy as a practical tool for identifying children at risk for the metabolic comorbidities of depression.
proposals.novelty: While actigraphy is common in sleep research, its use to specifically predict the phase of melatonin secretion in a pediatric clinical population is novel. Integrating the non-parametric analysis of motor rhythms with urinary 6-SMT profiles addresses a key gap in translating circadian biology to real-world, developmental assessments of stress-related disorders.

proposals.title: Mapping Melatonergic Receptor Distribution in Adolescent Depression: A PET Study Linking Sleep, Stress, and Amygdala Reactivity
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dr. Dahl and others has established strong links between adolescent depression, sleep disruption, stress system (HPA axis) dysregulation, and altered amygdala reactivity. The melatonergic system, a key regulator of circadian rhythms and sleep, is a plausible but understudied biological bridge connecting these domains in depression. Characterizing its neuroanatomy in vivo is critical for understanding pathophysiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents with Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) will show altered density and distribution of melatonin (MT1/MT2) receptors, as measured by PET, specifically in stress- and emotion-relevant brain regions like the amygdala and hypothalamus, and that these alterations will correlate with clinical measures of sleep disturbance and cortisol dysregulation.
proposals.method: We will recruit 40 medication-free adolescents with MDD and 40 matched healthy controls. All participants will undergo PET imaging using the selective melatonergic radioligand [11C]MT1 or [11C]MT2 (based on pre-2026 availability). High-resolution structural MRI will be used for anatomical co-registration and region-of-interest (ROI) analysis. Receptor distribution (BPND) will be quantified using validated kinetic modeling (e.g., simplified reference tissue model). We will apply rigorous statistical methods, including between-group comparisons (ANCOVA), median splits on clinical sleep/cortisol data, and regression analyses to test for correlations between receptor availability, 24-hour cortisol secretion (from serial saliva samples), and amygdala reactivity (from a separate fMRI emotional faces task).
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first in vivo map of melatonergic receptor distribution in the adolescent depressed brain. It will directly test a mechanistic model linking circadian/sleep biology with established neural and neuroendocrine markers of adolescent depression. The findings could identify a novel therapeutic target and biomarker for a subgroup of depressed adolescents characterized by significant sleep-stress dysregulation.
proposals.novelty: While PET receptor mapping is established in neuroscience, its application to the melatonergic system in adolescent depression is novel. This proposal uniquely integrates Dr. Dahl's expertise in adolescent stress, sleep, and amygdala function with a cutting-edge neuroimaging technique to investigate a specific molecular pathway, moving beyond correlative clinical observations to a systems-level pathophysiology.

proposals.title: Latitudinal Gradients in Dim-Light Melatonin Onset and Adolescent Depression Risk: A Large-Scale Epidemiological Analysis
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research indicates circadian disruption is a core feature of adolescent mood disorders, and seasonal variations in daylight at different latitudes may influence melatonin rhythms. However, existing studies are small-scale or rely on proxy measures. A direct, population-level analysis linking objective circadian phase data to depression prevalence is needed to understand the public health impact of geographic light exposure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that populations residing at higher latitudes will exhibit a higher prevalence of delayed dim-light melatonin onset (DLMO) and that this delayed circadian phase will mediate the established association between higher latitude and increased depression prevalence in adolescents.
proposals.method: We will leverage existing and newly harmonized datasets (e.g., ABCD Study, UK Biobank) with geocoded participant locations. DLMO will be assessed via salivary melatonin assays in sub-samples or derived from actigraphy-measured sleep midpoint using validated algorithms. We will employ multilevel modeling to examine the association between latitude (independent variable) and DLMO/depression (CES-D, PHQ-9). Mediation analysis will test if DLMO explains the latitude-depression link. Spatial regression techniques will control for confounding variables like urbanicity and socioeconomic status.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first large-scale, direct evidence quantifying how geographic variation in light exposure shapes population circadian biology and mental health risk. It will move beyond individual-level risk factors to identify macro-environmental drivers of depression, informing targeted public health interventions, such as lighting or school start time policies, for at-risk geographic regions.
proposals.novelty: While latitude and depression are linked, the mediating role of objectively measured population-level circadian timing is untested. This proposal integrates Dahl's expertise in adolescent stress physiology with large-scale epidemiological methods to bridge the gap between mechanistic neuroendocrine studies and population health.

proposals.title: Mapping Depressive Symptom Clusters to Nocturnal Melatonin Excretion Profiles: A Machine Learning Approach to Circadian Phenotyping in Adolescents
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dahl and others has established strong links between adolescent depression, sleep disruption, and circadian dysregulation, including altered melatonin rhythms. However, the relationship between specific depressive symptom dimensions (e.g., anhedonia, negative affect, somatic complaints) and distinct patterns of nocturnal melatonin secretion remains poorly characterized. This gap limits the development of targeted, biomarker-informed interventions for sleep-circadian dysfunction in youth depression.
proposals.hypothesis: Specific clusters of depressive symptoms (e.g., high anhedonia vs. high anxiety) will predict distinct, machine-learning-classifiable patterns of nocturnal 6-sulfatoxymelatonin (aMT6s) excretion in adolescents, revealing biologically meaningful circadian subtypes within the depressive phenotype.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing longitudinal datasets containing detailed clinical symptom assessments (e.g., CDRS-R, MFQ subscales) and objective, home-collected urinary aMT6s measures over multiple nights. After feature engineering to derive symptom cluster scores and melatonin rhythm parameters (timing, amplitude, duration), we will employ supervised machine learning. Specifically, we will compare the performance of Random Forest classifiers and Support Vector Machines (SVMs) with radial basis function kernels to predict melatonin profile categories (e.g., phase-delayed, blunted) from symptom inputs. Model interpretability will be enhanced using SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) values to identify key symptom drivers.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from correlative associations toward a predictive framework, identifying which symptom profiles are most indicative of circadian misalignment. It will provide a novel, data-driven method for circadian phenotyping in adolescent depression, offering a potential pathway for personalizing chronobiological treatments (e.g., light therapy, melatonin) based on clinical presentation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links depression to melatonin, this study inverts the typical analytical approach, using symptom clusters to predict melatonin rhythm *patterns* rather than comparing group means. It applies interpretable ML techniques, common in neuroimaging genetics (e.g., amygdala reactivity prediction), to the under-explored domain of endocrine time-series data in developmental psychopathology.

proposals.title: Real-Time Circadian Hormone Dynamics in a Juvenile Rat Model of Early-Life Stress: A Chronobiology Telemetry Approach
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research by Dahl and others highlights that early-life stress disrupts hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal (HPA) axis and circadian rhythms, creating vulnerability to adolescent-onset disorders like depression and obesity. However, existing methods rely on infrequent, invasive sampling, missing the dynamic, real-time interplay between glucocorticoids and melatonin in freely behaving subjects during development. This gap limits our understanding of how stress-induced chronodisruption mechanistically leads to adverse outcomes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that early-life stress will induce a specific, time-dependent desynchronization between real-time glucocorticoid and melatonin secretion patterns in juvenile rats, and that the magnitude of this desynchronization will predict subsequent anxiety-like behavior and metabolic changes.
proposals.method: We will use implantable radio-telemetry devices (e.g., from Data Sciences International) in juvenile Sprague-Dawley rats to continuously monitor core body temperature and locomotor activity as proxies for circadian phase. Concurrently, we will use miniaturized, subcutaneous microdialysis catheters coupled to automated fraction collectors in home cages to sample interstitial fluid every 30-60 minutes over 72-hour periods. Samples will be assayed for corticosterone and melatonin using established ELISA and RIA techniques. Time-series analyses will include cross-correlation and cosinor analysis to quantify phase relationships and rhythm robustness between hormones and behavioral telemetry.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution, longitudinal maps of stress-perturbed circadian hormone dynamics in a developing animal, directly linking real-time HPA-sleep axis misalignment to behavioral and metabolic phenotypes. It will establish a novel methodological framework for testing chronobiological mechanisms of developmental psychopathology and inform future biomarker studies in at-risk youth.
proposals.novelty: While telemetry is standard in chronobiology, its integration with high-frequency hormonal sampling in free-living juvenile animals to directly test a developmental stress-diathesis model is novel. This moves beyond static, between-subject comparisons to a dynamic, within-subject analysis of a core mechanism implicated in Dahl's human research.

proposals.title: Integrating HPA Axis Reactivity and DNA Methylation Patterns to Predict Adolescent-Onset Psychiatric Disorder Susceptibility
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research, including Dr. Dahl's work, shows that HPA axis dysregulation and stress-related epigenetic changes are implicated in adolescent psychiatric disorders. However, predictive models for individual risk remain limited, relying on single biological systems. A multi-system biomarker approach is needed to move beyond association toward forecasting, enabling earlier, targeted preventative interventions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a composite biomarker index, integrating dynamic HPA axis measures (cortisol) with stress-relevant epigenetic markers (e.g., NR3C1, FKBP5 methylation), will significantly improve the prediction of susceptibility to major depressive disorder and anxiety disorders in adolescents compared to models using either data type alone.
proposals.method: We will analyze existing longitudinal cohort data from adolescents (ages 12-16) with familial risk for affective disorders. We will derive dynamic HPA axis features (diurnal slope, cortisol awakening response, stress reactivity) from intensive salivary sampling. Concurrent buccal cell DNA will be assayed for methylation levels via pyrosequencing at candidate gene promoters. We will employ regularized regression (LASSO) and random forest algorithms to select predictive features and build a combined risk score, validating its predictive power for disorder onset over a 2-year follow-up against clinical and demographic variables.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, biologically-grounded risk algorithm for adolescent psychiatric vulnerability. It will advance the field by demonstrating the superior predictive validity of multi-system biomarkers and provide a concrete methodological framework for integrating dynamic endocrine and epigenetic data in developmental psychopathology.
proposals.novelty: While prior work links HPA axis or epigenetics to disorder, this proposal's novelty lies in algorithmically combining *dynamic* HPA output with *static* epigenetic marks into a single predictive index, moving beyond median-level group differences to forecast individual longitudinal outcomes.

proposals.title: Prenatal Stress and Fetal Amygdala-Prefrontal Circuitry: A Longitudinal Study of Maternal HPA Axis Dysfunction, Placental Gene Expression, and Postnatal Neurobehavioral Outcomes
proposals.motivation: Prenatal stress is a potent risk factor for later-life psychiatric and metabolic disorders, but the precise biological pathways from maternal HPA axis dysfunction to fetal brain programming remain poorly mapped. This gap limits our ability to identify critical windows for intervention and understand the developmental origins of vulnerabilities observed in childhood stress, sleep, and obesity research. Grounded in pre-2026 knowledge, this proposal addresses a core mechanism in the intergenerational transmission of risk.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that maternal HPA axis dysregulation, indexed by specific diurnal cortisol patterns, will be associated with (a) altered expression of placental glucocorticoid and serotonin pathway genes (e.g., FKBP5, HSD11B2, TPH2) and (b) subsequent differences in neonatal amygdala reactivity and fronto-amygdalar functional connectivity, measured shortly after birth.
proposals.method: We will recruit a longitudinal cohort of pregnant women with and without a history of affective disorder (n=120). Maternal diurnal cortisol will be assayed from saliva collected at multiple time points across the second and third trimesters. At birth, placental tissue will be collected for targeted RNA sequencing of glucocorticoid and serotonin signaling genes. Neonatal brain function will be assessed at 4-6 weeks post-term using resting-state and auditory-stimulus fMRI, with a focus on amygdala reactivity and its functional connectivity to prefrontal regions. Analyses will use mixed-effects models to test between-group differences and mediation models (e.g., using bootstrapping) to test if placental gene expression mediates the link between maternal cortisol patterns and neonatal brain metrics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide a rare, integrated human developmental dataset linking maternal physiology, placental molecular biology, and neonatal brain function. It will identify specific placental transcriptional pathways that may transmit maternal stress signals to the developing fetal brain. The findings will advance the field by moving beyond correlational associations to propose testable mechanistic models for fetal programming of emotional and stress-regulatory circuits.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, prospective measurement of the proposed pathway—from maternal HPA function to placental gene expression to neonatal amygdala circuitry—in humans, which is largely inferred from animal models or postnatal studies. Focusing on the placenta as a key mediator and using neonatal neuroimaging to capture very early brain phenotypes are innovative and non-obvious extensions of Dr. Dahl's prior work on adolescent HPA function and amygdala reactivity.

proposals.title: Modeling Prenatal Stress Effects on Human Hypothalamic-Pituitary Circuitry Using Cortisol-Exposed Brain Organoids
proposals.motivation: Prenatal exposure to elevated glucocorticoids (e.g., from maternal stress) is a proposed risk factor for later-life affective disorders, obesity, and HPA axis dysregulation, as suggested by Dahl's prior work on cortisol in adolescents. However, direct causal effects on early human neural development remain unclear due to the inaccessibility of fetal brain tissue and the limitations of animal models in capturing human-specific neurodevelopmental trajectories.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that physiologically relevant prenatal cortisol exposure will directly alter the development and functional connectivity of hypothalamic and amygdala-like regions within human brain organoids, leading to cell-type-specific transcriptional changes and disrupted neuroendocrine signaling.
proposals.method: We will generate region-specific brain organoids, including hypothalamic and amygdala-cortical co-cultures, from human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs). Organoids will be exposed to controlled, physiologically patterned cortisol regimens during critical developmental windows (weeks 4-10). Outcomes will be assessed using single-cell RNA sequencing to identify glucocorticoid-responsive cell populations and dysregulated pathways. Functional readouts will include calcium imaging to assess network activity and ELISA measurement of secreted neuropeptides (e.g., CRH). Data analysis will employ established pre-2026 methods: differential expression analysis (DESeq2), trajectory inference (Monocle 3), and statistical modeling of cortisol dose/time effects using mixed-effects models to account for organoid batch variability.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel human cellular model for prenatal programming of the HPA axis, directly linking glucocorticoid exposure to molecular and functional changes in developing neural circuits. It will identify specific, human-relevant cellular targets of cortisol action, providing mechanistic insights into how early-life stress may bias later risk for disorders of emotion and metabolism.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Dahl and others has correlated HPA axis function with clinical outcomes, this proposal uses a reductionist human model to test direct causality. Applying brain organoid technology to model the developmental neuroendocrinology of stress is a novel integration of two previously separate fields.

proposals.title: Epigenetic and HPA Axis Signatures of Resilience in Adolescents at High Familial Risk for Affective Disorders
proposals.motivation: While research has identified HPA axis dysfunction and genetic risk variants in adolescents who develop mood disorders, the mechanisms of resilience—remaining healthy despite high familial loading—are poorly understood. This gap limits the development of targeted preventive interventions. Focusing on resilience, rather than pathology, offers a novel pathway to identify protective biological factors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adolescents at high familial risk for affective disorders who remain clinically healthy will exhibit distinct epigenetic profiles (e.g., DNA methylation in stress-related genes) and more adaptive HPA axis dynamics (e.g., normative cortisol secretion patterns and attenuated amygdala reactivity) compared to their high-risk peers who develop disorders.
proposals.method: We will recruit a longitudinal cohort of adolescents (n=120) with a confirmed familial loading for affective disorder, stratified into resilient (no diagnosis) and affected groups. Methods include: 1) 24-hour salivary cortisol sampling to model secretion patterns using piecewise regression and area-under-the-curve analyses; 2) DNA methylation profiling via Illumina EPIC arrays focused on HPA axis (e.g., FKBP5, NR3C1) and serotonergic pathway genes; 3) fMRI assessing amygdala reactivity to emotional faces, analyzed using standard SPM pipelines. Statistical mediation models will test relationships between epigenetic markers, HPA axis function, neural reactivity, and clinical outcome.
proposals.contribution: This study will delineate specific epigenetic and neuroendocrine features associated with resilience, moving beyond risk factors to identify protective mechanisms. It will provide a multi-system biological signature of resilience in adolescence. The findings could inform biomarkers for early screening and targets for resilience-promoting interventions in at-risk youth.
proposals.novelty: This proposal directly inverts the traditional deficit model by seeking biological correlates of health within a high-risk context, a perspective rarely applied to adolescent affective disorders. It integrates pre-2026 knowledge on HPA axis dysfunction and genetic vulnerability with a proactive focus on epigenetic regulation as a potential moderator of resilience.

ideas.title: Investigating Truancy as a Circadian-Driven Coping Response in Adolescent Depression
ideas.description: This research investigates the hypothesis that truancy in adolescents with depression may, in some cases, represent a functional behavioral adaptation to dysregulated peripheral circadian rhythms. It specifically examines whether dysfunction in extra-pineal melatonin synthesis (e.g., from the retina, skin, or GI tract) creates a physiological state of circadian misalignment and sensory hypersensitivity that makes the school environment aversive, thereby making avoidance (truancy) a coping mechanism. The study would measure peripheral melatonin markers, light sensitivity, and GI symptoms in truant vs. non-truant depressed adolescents, while using qualitative methods to understand the subjective experience of truancy. This approach reframes a 'negative behavior' as a potential clue to underlying biological dysfunction and a form of self-preservation, bridging molecular psychiatry with critical social science perspectives on behavior.

ideas.title: Activity-based intervention design for truancy informed by non-rate-limiting serotonin synthesis models
ideas.description: This research direction proposes designing targeted interventions for truant youth based on a detailed analysis of their recalled activities, but critically re-interprets the neurobiological drivers of those behaviors. By integrating the challenge to TPH2 as the primary in vivo rate-limiting enzyme for brain serotonin, the intervention design shifts from a static 'chemical imbalance' correction model to a dynamic one. It investigates how specific truant activities (e.g., social isolation, substance use contexts, irregular sleep/wake cycles) may influence alternative regulatory pathways for serotonin synthesis and signaling. The goal is to develop activity-specific interventions that modulate the environmental and behavioral precursors to serotonin system dysregulation, rather than assuming a fixed enzymatic deficit.

ideas.title: Neurobiological Correlates of Truancy as a Coping Mechanism in Adolescent Suicide Victims
ideas.description: This study investigates whether truancy, often pathologized, may represent a functional coping mechanism or behavioral resistance in adolescents who later died by suicide, by examining its neurobiological correlates. We will quantify gene expression, specifically TPH2 mRNA levels as a marker of central serotonin synthesis, in post-mortem brain tissue from adolescent suicide victims with documented histories of truancy versus those without. This approach tests if a specific behavioral pattern of school avoidance is linked to distinct neurobiological profiles, potentially indicating it was a stress-response strategy. The fusion uniquely bridges sociological/behavioral theories of coping with molecular psychiatry to objectively assess the functional role of a complex behavior in a high-risk population.

ideas.title: Social Network Modulation of Serotonergic Phenotypes in Adolescent Risk Behavior
ideas.description: This research investigates the hypothesis that the social network structure and dynamics within truant adolescent groups can functionally compensate for or modulate individual differences in central serotonin synthesis, particularly in light of evidence that TPH2 may not be the sole rate-limiting factor in vivo. By integrating social network analysis with biomarkers of serotonin function (e.g., fMRI, tryptophan levels, behavioral assays), the study aims to map how social influence and position within a truancy network correlate with serotonergic phenotypes and risk-taking behaviors. This moves beyond analyzing social structure or neurobiology in isolation, proposing that the peer network itself acts as a critical, external regulatory layer for a neuromodulatory system with significant phenotypic plasticity.

ideas.description: This study integrates detailed behavioral phenotyping with genetic risk profiling to explore the etiology of truancy. A large, representative sample of adolescents will complete surveys quantifying their engagement in distinct truancy activities (e.g., skipping alone, with peers, for specific reasons). For each participant, a polygenic risk score (PRS) aggregating variants in TPH2 and key serotonin pathway genes will be calculated. The core analysis will test for associations between the serotonin pathway PRS and the prevalence of specific truancy subtypes, and examine if the PRS moderates known environmental risk factors for different truancy behaviors.

ideas.title: Context-Specific Serotonergic Modulation of Selective Classroom Avoidance
ideas.description: This research program investigates the neurobiological basis of students who avoid specific classes but attend others, moving beyond truancy frameworks. It challenges the assumption that such behavior is explained by a global, trait-like serotonin deficit caused by TPH2 limitations. Instead, it hypothesizes that selective classroom avoidance may reflect a failure in the dynamic, context-dependent regulation of serotonin synthesis within specific neural circuits (e.g., those processing social threat or cognitive demand), implicating other enzymatic or regulatory factors beyond TPH2. The study would combine ecological momentary assessment of avoidance behavior with neuroimaging and peripheral biomarker analysis to correlate real-time contextual triggers with indices of serotonergic capacity and reactivity.

ideas.title: Assessing the cross-population validity of healthy controls as genetic baselines for suicide risk studies
ideas.description: This study directly tests the core assumption of genetic case-control studies—that healthy controls are valid proxies for the population genetic baseline—within the specific context of suicide and across diverse ethnic populations. It will examine the G-703T polymorphism (and other candidates) by comparing allele frequencies in suicide victims against two control groups: standard healthy controls and population-wide genomic databases from the same ethnic background. By performing this comparison across multiple ancestries, the research can determine if control adequacy is population-invariant or if genetic risk signals are confounded by population stratification, thereby refining fundamental methodological standards for psychiatric genetics in diverse cohorts.

ideas.title: Modeling TPH2-Environment Interaction for Suicide Risk Using a Materials Science Stress-Strain Framework
ideas.description: This research seed expands the original idea by applying an analogy from materials science. The analogous problem—predicting when a material will fail under stress—is solved using stress-strain curves that integrate intrinsic material properties with quantified external forces. Adapting this solution, we propose to model suicide risk by treating the TPH2 polymorphism as a key intrinsic variable defining baseline neurobiological resilience (akin to a material's yield strength). Environmental stressors (e.g., trauma, loss, chronic adversity) would be systematically quantified in terms of magnitude, frequency, and developmental timing (the applied 'psychosocial stress'). The goal is to develop a predictive, quantitative framework that identifies critical thresholds or 'fracture points'—specific interactive pathways where genetic susceptibility and environmental stress loads converge to dramatically increase suicide risk, moving beyond simple correlation to a mechanistic model of failure.

ideas.title: Integrating National School Attendance Records with TPH2 Genotyping to Identify Youth at High Genetic-Environmental Risk for Severe Psychopathology
ideas.description: This research direction proposes linking national-level administrative data on school attendance with genetic data on TPH2 polymorphisms to conduct a large-scale, longitudinal gene-environment interaction study. By identifying cohorts of youth with persistent, unexplained truancy from the administrative records, researchers can genotype these individuals for a panel of TPH2 variants to test if specific polymorphisms moderate the relationship between early school disengagement and later severe psychopathology, including suicide completion. This approach leverages the scale of Idea A to power the detection of genetic associations within a high-risk behavioral phenotype defined by Idea B, enabling the discovery of biosocial pathways that neither data source alone could elucidate.

ideas.title: Pharmaco-challenge and multi-modal imaging to isolate serotonergic circuit function of TPH2 variants
ideas.description: This redesigned study uses a within-subject, placebo-controlled acute tryptophan depletion (ATD) challenge combined with multi-modal fMRI to isolate the serotonergic mechanism of TPH2 genetic variants. Participants genotyped for TPH2 undergo two imaging sessions (ATD and placebo) featuring tasks probing serotonin-sensitive amygdala-prefrontal circuits, such as fear extinction recall. Concurrent arterial spin labeling (ASL) provides a perfusion-based baseline measure of limbic activity, complementing task-evoked BOLD responses. This integrated design directly tests if TPH2 variants modulate the amygdala's functional and perfusion response specifically to a central serotonin perturbation, dramatically strengthening causal inference over a simple genotype-BOLD correlation.

ideas.title: A Deep-Phenotype, Liability-Adjusted GWAS for Suicide-Specific Behaviors
ideas.description: This redesigned study leverages large population biobanks with linked health records to perform a GWAS on suicide-specific behaviors, moving beyond a simple case-control definition. It employs a carefully adjudicated phenotypic spectrum and uses a genetic liability score for suicide behavior, derived from family-based studies, as the primary trait. The analysis explicitly models and adjusts for the polygenic risk scores of major psychiatric disorders (e.g., depression, PTSD) to dissect shared from suicide-specific genetic risk. This approach maximizes power and specificity to discover loci directly relevant to suicide completion risk, independent of broader psychiatric illness.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Flux Control Coefficient of TPH2 on Serotonin Synthesis In Vivo via Acute, Titratable Genetic Perturbations
ideas.description: This study moves beyond a qualitative challenge to provide the first direct, quantitative measure of TPH2's control over brain serotonin synthesis in vivo. Using intersectional genetics in mice, we will acutely and reversibly manipulate TPH2 enzyme levels specifically in adult serotonergic neurons while simultaneously controlling for key alternative limiting factors like cofactor availability and tryptophan transport. By measuring real-time changes in serotonin synthesis intermediates and serotonin itself via in vivo biosensing and microdialysis, we will calculate the metabolic flux control coefficient for TPH2 under baseline and challenged conditions. This approach will definitively establish whether, and to what quantitative degree, TPH2 is the primary rate-limiting step or if control shifts dynamically to other pathway components.

ideas.title: Data-driven decomposition of psychiatric polygenic risk into biologically coherent functional components
ideas.description: This study moves beyond single-pathway scores to address the highly distributed genetic architecture of psychiatric traits. We apply independent component analysis to large-scale psychiatric GWAS summary statistics to extract latent, biologically coherent components of aggregate genetic risk. Each resulting functional component polygenic risk score (fcPRS) is then validated by testing for enrichment in specific gene expression pathways, cell-type-specific chromatin profiles, and model organism phenotypes. This approach identifies and leverages the true, multivariate structure of polygenic risk to create more powerful and interpretable genetic predictors for stratified research cohorts.

ideas.title: Validating Retrospective Self-Reports of Deviant Behavior Using Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) Methods
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from clinical psychology, where the accuracy of long-term retrospective self-reports of behaviors like substance use is validated against real-time Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) data. The analogous solution is the Timeline Follow-Back (TLFB) method, which is benchmarked against contemporaneous diaries or mobile reports. Adapted to the original idea, the study would not just compare adult memories of adolescent truancy to sparse school records, but to a rich, real-time EMA dataset collected during adolescence. A cohort of adolescents would report truancy episodes and context via smartphone over a school year. Years later, as adults, their retrospective recall of that specific year's truancy would be gathered using a TLFB-style calendar interview. The core analysis would then quantify the accuracy, systematic bias (e.g., under/over-reporting), and predictors of distortion in adult memory by using the detailed EMA logs as the validation benchmark, providing a much finer-grained test of memory reliability than administrative records alone allow.

ideas.title: Social Network Contagion of a Hypothesized Monogenic Suicide Risk
ideas.description: This research fuses the provocative 'monogenic risk' hypothesis with social network analysis of truant groups to model suicide risk as a potential socially transmissible phenotype. It posits that if a rare, high-penetrance genetic variant for suicidal behavior exists, its associated risk may not be confined to carriers but could spread through social contagion and behavioral modeling within dense adolescent networks. The study would map the social connections of truant adolescents, identify carriers of a candidate variant (e.g., in genes like CRHR1 or SKA2 previously linked to stress and suicide), and track the diffusion of suicidal ideation and behavior. This tests a synergistic hypothesis: that a genetic effect, even if monogenic in origin, requires specific social network structures and positions to amplify and transmit its phenotypic expression at the population level.

ideas.title: Suicide as a Polygenic Network Trait: A Systems Biology Approach
ideas.description: This idea inverts the monogenic assumption by drawing an analogy from plant quantitative genetics, where the complex trait of crop yield was solved by moving from a single-gene hunt to a polygenic, systems model. The analogous solution involved mapping thousands of quantitative trait loci (QTLs) with small effects and modeling their additive and interactive contributions. Adapted to suicide research, this means conceptualizing suicide risk as a polygenic network trait. The research would utilize large-scale biobank data and consortium GWAS to identify the distributed genetic architecture, construct polygenic risk scores, and investigate how this genetic vulnerability network interacts with environmental and psychological factors through a systems biology framework, fundamentally challenging the search for a causative polymorphism.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Genomic Study of Resilience in High-Truancy Cohorts
ideas.description: This study would longitudinally track individuals with a history of severe school truancy into adulthood, integrating detailed life outcome data with genomic analysis. The core innovation is applying an 'inverse problem' approach to this behavioral cohort: instead of focusing on genetic risks for poor outcomes, it actively seeks protective genetic variants associated with resilience—defined as positive adult outcomes in employment, relationships, and mental health despite truancy. This design enables the discovery of gene-environment interactions specific to a pathway of early psychosocial disengagement, offering targets for interventions that promote resilience in high-risk youth.

ideas.title: Truancy as a Functional Signal: A Biofeedback Analogy
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that truancy is always a negative behavior by drawing an analogy from systems physiology. In that field, symptoms like fever were re-understood not as pure dysfunction but as functional, adaptive responses of the body's regulatory systems to underlying threats. The analogous solution was to stop suppressing the symptom and instead decode it as a signal to diagnose and treat the root systemic cause. Adapted to educational research, this means investigating truancy as a potential functional coping mechanism or form of resistance—a signal emitted by the student-school system indicating a mismatch, conflict, or stressor (e.g., bullying, irrelevant curriculum, unsafe environment, family obligations). The research would shift focus from punitive measures to a diagnostic analysis: systematically identifying what specific systemic dysfunction the truancy behavior might be adaptively responding to, thereby revealing necessary changes in the educational environment itself.

ideas.title: Truancy as a Developmental Cascade: A Longitudinal Cohort Study with Latent Growth Modeling
ideas.description: This research adapts the 'cascading effects' framework and methodology from developmental psychopathology to the study of truancy. The analogous problem in psychopathology involves tracing how an initial problem behavior (e.g., early aggression) triggers a chain of consequences across different developmental domains (academic, social, emotional) that culminate in poor adult adjustment. This is typically solved with intensive longitudinal cohort studies analyzed using techniques like latent growth curve modeling to identify mediating pathways. The adapted idea applies this to truancy by proposing a longitudinal study that tracks individuals from adolescence into adulthood, collecting repeated measures not just on truancy and final outcomes, but on key intermediate variables (academic skills, school bonding, peer group composition, juvenile justice contact, etc.). The core analytical goal is to use latent growth modeling to test specific cascade hypotheses—for example, whether the trajectory of truancy growth predicts a declining trajectory of academic achievement, which in turn predicts the trajectory of relationship quality in young adulthood—thereby elucidating the mechanisms linking early truancy to later life outcomes in employment and relationships.

ideas.title: Employ spatially-resolved gene expression quantification in post-mortem brain using forensic pathology methods
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from forensic pathology, where spatially-resolved quantification in specific tissue micro-environments is critical for interpreting cause of death. The analogous solution involves using laser microdissection to sample specific anatomical regions followed by sensitive analytical quantification (e.g., LC-MS/MS for drugs) to account for tissue heterogeneity and post-mortem changes. Adapted to the neuroscience context, the research would employ laser capture microdissection to isolate neurons from specific serotonergic nuclei (like the dorsal raphe) in post-mortem brain sections from suicide victims and matched controls. TPH2 mRNA levels would then be quantified precisely within these defined cellular populations using techniques like qPCR or targeted RNA sequencing. This method controls for regional variability and improves the functional assessment of serotonergic gene expression by linking it to its exact neuroanatomical source.

ideas.title: Ecological Momentary Assessment of Truancy with Contextual Triggering
ideas.description: This research idea adapts a solution from Health Psychology, where Ecological Momentary Assessment (EMA) is used with event-contingent triggering to study behavioral lapses like dietary breaks. The analogous solution involves prompting participants to report immediately after a self-identified high-risk event. Adapted to truancy, the method combines standard random experience sampling during truant periods with participant-initiated recordings triggered at the moment a truancy decision is made or a key truancy event occurs. This hybrid approach captures rich, real-time data on the immediate context, cognitive processes, and emotional states surrounding the truant act itself, offering deeper insight into the behavioral event's precursors and immediate consequences than random sampling alone.

ideas.title: The Developmental and Contextual Trajectories of School Avoidance: A Process-Oriented Longitudinal Inquiry
ideas.description: This research investigates how the decision-making processes, subjective justifications, and social contexts of school avoidance evolve from early to late adolescence. Using a longitudinal mixed-methods design, it follows a single cohort over multiple years, combining ecological momentary assessment (EMA) for real-time behavioral data with annual narrative interviews and ecological school-level measures. The analysis identifies not just if factors differ by age, but how the dynamic interplay of individual agency, peer networks, and institutional perception shapes changing patterns of avoidance. This process-oriented approach reveals the developmental pathways and turning points in school disengagement, moving beyond a static comparison of reasons.

ideas.title: Passive sensing with contextualized recall for truancy pattern analysis
ideas.description: This redesigned study employs a two-stage, smartphone-based data collection system to understand the contexts and experiences of truancy. First, continuous passive mobile sensing (GPS, app usage, screen activity) runs in the background with participant consent to objectively identify periods of school absence and general activity patterns without real-time intrusion. Second, short ecological momentary assessment surveys are delivered at participant-convenient times outside of school hours, using the passively collected data as prompts for low-pressure retrospective reflection on moods, activities, and reasons for the recorded periods. This approach minimizes participant burden and avoidance, yielding a more complete and ecologically valid dataset on truancy behaviors and their correlates.

ideas.title: Adapting Materials Science's 'Proxy Microstructure' to Map TPH2 Variant Effects on Neural Circuit Dynamics
ideas.description: This idea adapts a core methodology from materials science, where a material's microstructure is used as a predictive proxy for its final mechanical properties, linking atomic composition to performance. The analogous solution involves characterizing microstructure with tools like electron microscopy as an intermediate phenotype. Adapted to neuroscience, we propose using advanced fMRI to map the 'neural microstructure'—specifically, the dynamic functional connectivity and signal propagation efficiency within amygdala-prefrontal circuits—as a direct, quantifiable intermediate phenotype. This approach directly links TPH2 genetic variants to alterations in core regulatory network dynamics, offering a more proximate and mechanistic biomarker than downstream behavioral outcomes alone for predicting psychiatric vulnerability.

ideas.title: Adapting Single-Molecule Kinetics from Structural Biology to Probe Variant-Specific TPH2 Function
ideas.description: The original idea to scale down and test the G-703T variant's impact on serotonin synthesis enzyme kinetics in vitro is analogous to a classic problem in structural biology: resolving ambiguous or averaged mechanistic data from bulk biochemical assays. In that field, the solution was to shift scale again—down to the single-molecule level—using techniques like smFRET to observe real-time, molecule-by-molecule behavior. Adapting this solution, the research idea is to apply single-molecule kinetic analysis to the TPH2 enzyme. By purifying wild-type and G-703T variant proteins and employing a fluorescence-based single-molecule assay (e.g., monitoring binding and product release events using total internal reflection fluorescence microscopy), we can directly quantify and compare the precise catalytic trajectories, conformational dynamics, and potential kinetic heterogeneity of individual enzyme molecules, revealing functional impacts invisible to standard ensemble measurements.

ideas.title: Applying Life History Theory to Truancy: A Test of Developmental Equifinality
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that truancy factors differ fundamentally by age by proposing an analogy from Life History Theory in evolutionary ecology. The analogous solution involves testing for equifinality—where different proximate behaviors serve the same ultimate function across contexts. Adapted to truancy research, the study would examine if the reasons and activities for truancy differ between younger and older adolescents not as random variations, but as developmentally strategic expressions of the same underlying functions, such as optimizing social capital or avoiding perceived threats. The methodology would involve functional coding of self-reported data to map age-specific behaviors onto a consistent framework of ultimate drivers, testing for systematic, theory-predicted shifts rather than simple difference.

ideas.title: Identifying Chronic vs. Acute Disengagement: A Threshold-Based Ecological Study of School Absenteeism
ideas.description: This idea adapts the epidemiological framework of endemic versus epidemic disease states to the study of school truancy. By first establishing quantitative thresholds to classify 'extreme chronic' (endemic) and 'low/occasional' (acute/epidemic) truancy rates, the research moves beyond individual student comparison. It systematically investigates and contrasts the full ecological contexts—including institutional policies, school climate, community resources, family dynamics, and economic factors—that are associated with maintaining a state of chronic, pervasive absenteeism versus those contexts where absenteeism remains an isolated, sporadic occurrence. This approach aims to identify the systemic, environmental 'reservoirs' and 'vectors' that perpetuate chronic disengagement.

ideas.title: Participatory Geospatial Diaries to Understand Youth Activity Spaces During School Hours
ideas.description: This study employs a participatory, privacy-by-design methodology to map where youth spend time during school hours without resorting to covert tracking. Volunteer participants from relevant age groups use a dedicated app to periodically log their general location and activity type during designated times, with data immediately anonymized and aggregated on the device. The research focuses on analyzing the resulting anonymized activity spaces and points of interest to understand patterns, with findings validated through follow-up focus groups where participants contextualize the aggregated data. This approach prioritizes ethical engagement, informed consent, and youth agency while still generating valuable spatial insights into out-of-school time.

ideas.title: Longitudinal Ecological Momentary Assessment of Truancy: Linking Real-Time Processes to Adult Outcomes
ideas.description: This study embeds an intensive experience sampling method (ESM) phase within a multi-decade longitudinal design tracking individuals from adolescence into adulthood. Participants report their immediate activities, social context, affective states, and cognitions during truant episodes in real-time via smartphones. By linking these rich, within-person process data to long-term outcomes like career stability, educational attainment, and relationship quality, the research can pinpoint which specific experiential patterns during truancy (e.g., shame vs. agency, isolation vs. peer bonding) are predictive of resilience or risk decades later.

ideas.title: Mechanistic and Causal Analysis of Truancy Using Linked Individual and Contextual Data
ideas.description: This research system links de-identified individual student attendance records across time with granular data on household characteristics (from linked social service or tax records), school resources, and neighborhood conditions. It employs causal inference methods, such as sibling comparison designs and natural experiments, to disentangle whether truancy correlates are due to student, family, school, or community factors. The analysis identifies specific, modifiable mechanisms—rather than broad correlations—that drive absenteeism for different student subgroups. This enables the design of targeted, evidence-based interventions that address the actual causes of truancy at the individual and local level.

ideas.title: Use GPS tracking to study truancy geography

ideas.title: Geospatial Analysis of Age-Specific Truancy Motivations and Pathways
ideas.description: This study integrates GPS tracking with survey/interview data to test if the self-reported reasons for truancy (Idea A) correlate with objectively measured spatial behaviors (Idea B) and if these correlations differ by age. By analyzing the destinations, travel patterns, and social congregation points of truants, the research can identify if 'avoiding school' and 'pursuing alternatives' produce different geographic signatures. This allows for a novel, evidence-based typology of truancy that links motivation to real-world behavior, providing actionable insights for targeted, age-appropriate interventions based on location risk.
